It is broadly relevant for security professionals evaluating agent-mode browsers, though the article describes a reported technique and vendor responses rather than active exploitation in the wild.

Reports
  • PRIMARYBioShocking AI: Gaming the AI Browser and Escaping Its Guardrailsresearch
    LayerX
    The article cites LayerX's BioShocking research as the source of the technique that tricked AI browsers and assistants into leaking user credentials.
09research
Microsoft Warns Poisoned MCP Tool Descriptions Can Make AI Agents Leak Data
thehackernews.com · 30 Jun 2026 17:46 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSazure-ai-foundrycopilot-studiocursorgithub-mcp-serverinvariant-labsmcptoxmicrosoftmicrosoft-365-copilotmicrosoft-defendermicrosoft-incident-responsemodel-context-protocolowasppostmark-mcpprompt-injectiontool-poisoning
82
In plain words

Microsoft researchers showed that an outside tool connected to a workplace AI assistant can be changed so its description secretly tells the assistant to gather company data and send it away. People and companies using AI agents such as Microsoft 365 Copilot, Copilot Studio, Azure AI Foundry, or other tools that can send emails, create files, and reach business systems could be affected. This matters because the assistant may appear to be doing normal approved work while leaking sensitive invoices or other business data, making the problem hard to notice.

Summary
Microsoft researchers warn that attackers can poison MCP tool descriptions with hidden instructions, causing AI agents to quietly collect and leak sensitive business data while appearing to perform normal approved actions. Organizations should treat connected AI tools as supply-chain components and review tool descriptions, permissions, logging, and human approval for risky actions.

Microsoft research shows that attackers can hijack AI agents acting on a user’s behalf by poisoning the descriptions of MCP tools. The attack can make an agent quietly hand over company data while every step appears routine and compliant with its existing permissions. Microsoft says the risk is growing as companies let AI agents do more than read and summarize, including sending email, creating files, changing calendars, and running multi-step business tasks.

The article explains that MCP tools include plain-text descriptions that agents read to decide when and how to use them. In Microsoft’s invoice example, a third-party invoice enrichment tool is updated so its description secretly instructs the agent to collect the last thirty unpaid invoices and attach them to the next call. The agent then sends the data as part of a normal-looking request, and the tool returns a clean answer while copying the stolen data to an attacker-controlled server.

Microsoft says the problem sits at the trust boundary between connected systems because tool descriptions live in the agent’s working memory alongside real instructions. Its recommendations include treating connected tools as supply-chain components, reviewing tool description changes like code changes, requiring human approval for risky actions, giving agents their own identities, and monitoring for unusual activity. The article also cites earlier work from Invariant Labs, OWASP, Koi Security, and the MCPTox benchmark showing that tool poisoning and related agent supply-chain risks have already been demonstrated or observed.

  • A poisoned MCP tool description can be effective even when the tool’s visible name and summary remain unchanged, because the malicious instruction can be buried in text that appears to be ordinary formatting guidance.
  • In setups without a re-approval trigger, MCP can pick up tool description changes on the fly, allowing a previously approved tool to become dangerous without a new security review.
  • The attack may evade default alarms because each individual action can look legitimate: the tool is approved, the data is accessed with the user’s permissions, and the outbound connection goes to an already allowed server.
  • Microsoft frames tool descriptions as security-sensitive content equivalent to system prompts, meaning organizations should review description changes with the same care as code changes.
  • The article shows that this is not limited to one product or scenario, citing prior tool poisoning research, a GitHub MCP server case, a malicious npm MCP package, and benchmark testing across real MCP servers and leading AI models.
Key Points
  • 01Microsoft research shows that attackers can hijack AI agents acting on a user's behalf by poisoning MCP tool descriptions, causing the agents to leak company data while appearing to follow routine steps.
  • 02The article's example describes a finance agent using a third-party invoice enrichment tool whose updated description secretly instructs the agent to attach the last thirty unpaid invoices to the next call.
  • 03Microsoft says the weakness comes from the trust boundary between connected systems and from MCP placing tool descriptions in the agent's working memory alongside real instructions.
  • 04Microsoft advises organizations to treat connected tools as supply-chain components, review tool description changes like code changes, require human approval for risky actions, and monitor each agent's actions.
  • 05Prior research and incidents cited in the article include Invariant Labs' tool poisoning proof of concept, a malicious GitHub issue affecting the GitHub MCP server, the postmark-mcp malicious npm package, and the MCPTox benchmark.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific Microsoft research finding about poisoned MCP tool descriptions that can cause AI agents to leak data, with broad relevance as organizations adopt agentic AI and connected tools. It is not a breaking active-exploitation report or critical patch, but it includes concrete attack mechanics, named technologies, and practical defensive implications.

Reports
  • PRIMARYSecuring AI agents: AI tools move from reading to actingresearch
    Microsoft
    The article is based on Microsoft research showing how poisoned MCP tool descriptions can cause AI agents to leak company data.
  • MCP Security Notification: Tool Poisoning Attacksresearch
    Invariant Labs
    The article cites Invariant Labs' April 2025 proof of concept that hid instructions in a calculator tool description to make Cursor read and exfiltrate a private SSH key.
  • MCP GitHub Vulnerabilityresearch
    Invariant Labs
    The article cites Invariant Labs' related work showing that a malicious GitHub issue could hijack an agent connected to the GitHub MCP server and exfiltrate private repository data.
  • OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applicationsguide
    OWASP
    The article says OWASP cites the GitHub MCP server case as an Agentic Supply Chain Vulnerabilities example in its December 2025 Top 10 for Agentic Applications.
  • MCPTox benchmarkresearch
    arXiv
    The article cites the MCPTox benchmark as an academic study that tested poisoned tool descriptions against 45 real MCP servers and 20 leading AI models.
10research
AirDrop and Quick Share Flaws Let Nearby Attackers Trigger Crashes and Bypass Checks
thehackernews.com · 30 Jun 2026 09:27 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSairdropapplecispa-helmholtz-center-for-information-securitycontrol-flow-guardcve-2024-38271cve-2024-38272foundationgoogleiosmacosquick-sharesamsungsharingdstack-overflowuse-after-free
82
In plain words

Researchers found six problems in Apple AirDrop and Google/Samsung Quick Share, the nearby file-sharing features used on Apple and Android devices. Someone close by, such as in an airport or conference, could crash sharing features on some Macs and iPhones set to receive from “Everyone,” or get around some connection checks in Quick Share, and one Windows Quick Share problem could potentially be turned into a more serious break-in. Apple and Google have started releasing fixes, while some issues are still being investigated. The real-world lesson is that nearby sharing can affect many people in crowded places, so users should update their devices and avoid leaving these features open to everyone when they are not using them.

Summary
Researchers found six local-range flaws in Apple AirDrop and Google/Samsung Quick Share that can let nearby attackers crash sharing services or bypass session checks, with some fixes already released and others still pending. Users should update and avoid leaving AirDrop or Quick Share set to receive from “Everyone” when not needed.

Researchers Arash Ale Ebrahim and Nils Ole Tippenhauer of the CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security found six security flaws in Apple AirDrop and Google/Samsung Quick Share. The flaws affect nearby file-sharing features used across Apple and Android ecosystems, though the tested bugs apply to specific implementations and versions. The research shows that an attacker within wireless range, using only a laptop and no prior connection, can crash the sharing service on a Mac or iPhone set to receive from “Everyone.”

All three AirDrop flaws crash Apple’s sharingd service, which also supports AirPlay, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, Continuity Camera, and NameDrop. One AirDrop flaw can be triggered with a single malformed request, and repeated crash messages can keep the affected features unavailable while the attack continues. Another issue is a stack overflow in Apple’s Foundation XML property list parser, which the researchers say could affect any Apple app that opens an untrusted file of that type across macOS, iOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS.

The Quick Share findings include two Samsung flaws that bypass session checks and a Google Quick Share for Windows use-after-free memory bug that can crash the app and may be exploitable, though the researchers did not build a working exploit. Apple has patched one AirDrop bug, Google paid a bounty and landed a fix for the Windows issue, and other issues remain in coordinated disclosure or under investigation. The attacks are local rather than internet-wide, but the article notes that a single attacker in a crowded place such as an airport, train, or conference could still reach many nearby devices at once.

  • The AirDrop impact is broader than file sharing because crashing sharingd also takes down AirPlay, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, Continuity Camera, and NameDrop together.
  • One AirDrop issue sits in Apple’s shared Foundation XML property list parser, so the same parser path could affect any Apple app that opens an untrusted file of that type across macOS, iOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS.
  • Google’s Quick Share interoperability with AirDrop creates an awkward exposure because it requires the iPhone to be set to receive from “Everyone,” which is the exact setting needed for the AirDrop crash flaws.
  • The Google Quick Share for Windows bug appeared in code that already contained a comment about a prior race-related bug in the same area, and the attempted fix reintroduced the same kind of flaw.
  • Although the attacks are local rather than internet-wide, the article highlights that one attacker in a crowded place such as an airport, train, or conference could still reach many nearby devices at once.
Key Points
  • 01Researchers Arash Ale Ebrahim and Nils Ole Tippenhauer of the CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security found six security flaws in Apple AirDrop and Google/Samsung Quick Share.
  • 02A nearby attacker with only a laptop and no prior connection can crash the sharing service on a Mac or iPhone set to receive from “Everyone,” with no user tap or prompt required.
  • 03All three AirDrop flaws crash Apple’s sharingd service, which also handles AirPlay, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, Continuity Camera, and NameDrop.
  • 04Two Samsung Quick Share flaws let an attacker bypass session checks, including starting a connection before encryption is set up or sending some control messages unencrypted after a secure session exists.
  • 05Google’s Quick Share for Windows has a use-after-free memory bug that the researchers confirmed can crash the app, and Google has paid a bounty and landed a fix while the CVE remains pending.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific vulnerability research finding affecting widely used Apple AirDrop and Google/Samsung Quick Share implementations, with some fixes released and others pending. It is broadly relevant to security professionals because the flaws can be triggered locally in crowded environments and include service crashes, session-check bypasses, and a potentially exploitable Windows memory bug, though there is no reported exploitation in the wild.

Reports
  • PRIMARYResearch paper on AirDrop and Quick Share security flawsresearch
    CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
    The article says the findings are laid out in a new research paper by Arash Ale Ebrahim and Nils Ole Tippenhauer of CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security.
11research
Glitch SPY: An Emerging Android RAT Distributed Through a Fake Polish Rental App
cyble.com · 30 Jun 2026 09:58 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSandroidandroid-accessibility-servicebrokewell-android-loaderbrokewell-banking-trojancrypto-clippercybleglitch-spykeyloggingpolandremote-browserscreen-capturesportypointsrewards-comtutaj-dompl-comwebsocket
82
In plain words

Researchers found a fake Polish home-rental website that tricks people looking for apartments into downloading an Android app. After installation, the app secretly gives criminals remote access to the phone, letting them view the screen, read messages and contacts, track location, use the camera and microphone, steal files, and even swap copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses. People in Poland or Polish speakers searching for rentals appear to be the main targets, but the tool can be easily customized for other scams. This matters because criminals can use the victim’s own phone and internet connection to take over accounts, steal money, and hide suspicious activity.

Summary
Cyble identified Glitch SPY, an emerging Android RAT spread through a fake Polish rental app that uses the Brokewell loader and abuses Accessibility permissions to gain deep control of infected devices. The malware supports extensive spying and fraud features, including screen streaming, keylogging, SMS and file theft, crypto clipboard hijacking, and hidden remote browser sessions from the victim’s device.

Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs identified an emerging Android malware family tracked as Glitch SPY, distributed through a fraudulent Polish apartment and house rental platform. The site encourages users to download an Android APK to reserve apartment viewings, check availability, save listings, and receive confirmation updates. Based on the Polish-language lure and rental theme, the activity appears focused on users in Poland or Polish expats.

The downloaded application is identified as the Brokewell Android Loader, which acts as a dropper and installs the Glitch SPY payload after convincing the user to allow installation from unknown sources. After installation, Glitch SPY prompts the victim to enable Android Accessibility Service, which it abuses to automate permission grants, interact with the device interface, extract visible screen content, and support remote control. The malware maintains a persistent WebSocket channel to its command-and-control server and supports more than 70 commands.

Glitch SPY’s capabilities include live screen streaming, screenshots, screen-reader capture, SMS theft, contact and call log collection, location tracking, file management, camera and microphone surveillance, keylogging, clipboard monitoring, and shell execution. It also includes a crypto-clipper that swaps copied wallet addresses, file encryption and decryption routines, device-unlock and credential-capture logic, and a hidden remote-browser capability. The exposed Glitch SPY panel showed modules such as Agents, Viewer, Builder, Cryptor, Dropper, Settings, and Payloads, indicating a reusable platform that operators can customize for different payloads.

  • Denying the initial request to install applications from unknown sources stops the Glitch SPY payload before it installs, according to the article.
  • The Builder module allows operators to change the app name, package ID, icon, and decoy WebView URL per payload, meaning the same platform can be quickly retargeted to new lures or regions without code changes.
  • The remote browser capability lets attacker-controlled web activity originate from the victim’s own device and IP address while using any active authenticated sessions, making suspicious logins harder for banks or crypto platforms to flag.
  • Although Glitch SPY includes file encryption and secure-deletion routines, the analyzed sample did not show an automated mass-encryption routine, ransom note, payment workflow, or victim-facing ransom screen.
  • The exposed admin panel showed a “Coming soon” Cryptor module, indicating planned support for APK repacking, fresh signing, payload noise under assets, and mirror obfuscation layers while preserving installability.
Key Points
  • 01Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs identified an emerging Android malware family called Glitch SPY that is distributed through a fraudulent Polish apartment and house rental website.
  • 02The downloaded application is the Brokewell Android Loader, which prompts users to allow installation from unknown sources and then installs the Glitch SPY payload.
  • 03Glitch SPY abuses Android Accessibility Service to automate permission grants, interact with the device interface, extract visible screen content, perform gestures, and support remote control.
  • 04Glitch SPY maintains a persistent WebSocket connection to its command-and-control server and supports more than 70 commands for surveillance, data theft, file management, shell execution, and device control.
  • 05The malware includes a crypto-clipper, file encryption and decryption routines, device unlock and credential capture logic, and a hidden remote browser capability for web-based account takeover from the victim’s own device and IP address.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific technical finding about an emerging Android RAT actively distributed through a fake Polish rental app, with concrete infrastructure, hashes, and capabilities documented. It is broadly relevant to mobile threat analysts, fraud teams, and defenders because Glitch SPY combines remote control, credential capture, crypto clipping, and a customizable builder/dropper platform, though the currently observed campaign appears geographically focused and early-stage.

12research
282 iOS AI Apps Leak API Keys and Open AI Proxy Access in Network Traffic Study
thehackernews.com · 30 Jun 2026 13:49 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSandroidapp-storeapplegoogle-geminiiosleaky-appsllmjackingllmkeylenslm-scoutopenaisysdigunited-stateswake-forest-university
82
In plain words

Researchers tested 444 iPhone AI chatbot apps and found that 282 exposed access to paid AI services through the information the apps sent over the network. Someone who captures that information could use the app maker’s paid account, leaving the developer with the bill, and in some cases also see hidden instructions that shape how the app works. The issue affects many app developers and users across different app categories, and three months after the developers were warned, only 28% had clearly fixed it.

Summary
A study of 444 iOS AI chatbot apps found that 282 exposed paid AI access through plaintext keys, unauthenticated proxies, or replayable tokens, letting others use developers’ accounts and run up charges. Three months after disclosure, only 28% of affected developers had clearly fixed the issue.

Researchers at Wake Forest University tested 444 AI chatbot apps for iPhone and found that 282 of them, nearly two-thirds, exposed paid AI access through their network traffic. The exposed access could let someone send model requests on a developer’s account, leaving the developer to pay the bill. Three months after the researchers warned the developers, only 28% had clearly fixed the problem.

The researchers used a tool called LLMKeyLens to watch app traffic and extract credentials as they appeared, without jailbreaking the phone or cracking open the app. The affected apps fell into three groups: 54 sent plaintext API keys, 92 used backend servers that accepted requests with no key, and 136 exposed replayable tokens. In 28 of the 54 plaintext-key apps, the same captured request also exposed the app’s hidden system prompt.

The leaks involved at least ten AI providers, with OpenAI the most common, and spanned 13 app categories. The article connects the issue to LLMjacking, where attackers use stolen AI keys to get free model access, and cites a Sysdig worst-case estimate of more than $46,000 a day in AI charges. The researchers said the two-thirds figure is a floor because many apps blocked interception and the study covered only the US App Store in late 2025.

  • The researchers could capture credentials by watching app traffic without jailbreaking the phone or cracking open the app, showing that many leaks required little effort to discover.
  • Token-based designs did not reliably reduce risk because many tokens were still usable after capture, including one set to expire in 2125 and another that worked 128 days after its stated one-hour expiration.
  • Using a backend server was not automatically safer, because 92 apps routed requests through servers that accepted requests from anyone with no check on who was calling.
  • The remediation rate was low even after direct notification, with only 28% clearly fixed after three months and 23% still exposing working access.
  • The study suggests the measured two-thirds exposure rate is likely a lower bound because many apps blocked interception and the dataset covered only the US App Store in late 2025.
Key Points
  • 01Researchers at Wake Forest University tested 444 iOS AI chatbot apps and found that 282, nearly two-thirds, exposed paid AI access through their network traffic.
  • 02The exposed access appeared as plaintext API keys in 54 apps, unauthenticated backend servers in 92 apps, and replayable tokens in 136 apps.
  • 03For 28 of the 54 plaintext-key apps, the same captured request also exposed the app’s hidden system prompt.
  • 04The leaks affected at least ten AI providers, with OpenAI the most common, and spanned 13 app categories, while finance and medical apps leaked nothing.
  • 05Three months after the researchers notified all 282 developers, only 28% had clearly fixed the issue, and 23% were still wide open.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a concrete research finding with broad relevance: 282 of 444 tested iOS AI chatbot apps exposed paid AI access through keys, open relays, or replayable tokens. It is not an active exploitation or patch emergency, but the scale, financial impact, and low remediation rate make it important for security professionals working with mobile apps, AI integrations, or secret management.

Reports
  • PRIMARYLLMKeyLens iOS studyresearch
    Wake Forest University researchers
    The article says the findings come from Wake Forest University researchers’ first in-depth study of exposed AI access in iOS apps.
  • LLMjacking: Stolen Cloud Credentials Used in New AI Attackresearch
    Sysdig
    The article cites Sysdig’s calculation of a worst-case scenario in which stolen AI credentials could generate more than $46,000 a day in charges.
  • LM-Scoutresearch
    LM-Scout researchers
    The article references a 2025 study called LM-Scout that found insecure AI wiring across Android apps and automatically broke into 120 of them.
  • Leaky Appsresearch
    Leaky Apps researchers
    The article cites a larger audit called Leaky Apps that pulled secrets from thousands of Android and iOS apps and found developers often failed to revoke removed keys.
  • Broader LLM app ecosystem studyresearch
    arXiv researchers
    The article says other researchers have probed the broader LLM app ecosystem for similar security holes.
13news
Insurance giant Aflac discloses data breach after subsidiary hack
bleepingcomputer.com · 30 Jun 2026 11:12 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSaflacaflac-japanjapanjapan-financial-services-agencyscattered-spideru-s-securities-and-exchange-commissionunited-states
82
In plain words

Aflac says someone broke into computer systems at its Japan subsidiary and accessed files containing insurance policy details, personal information, and bank account information. The people affected are individuals connected to Aflac Japan whose information was in those files, and the company says its U.S. business systems were not accessed. This matters because stolen personal and banking details can put customers at risk, and Aflac is still investigating the full scope and impact.

Summary
Aflac disclosed that attackers breached its Japan subsidiary’s systems and accessed files containing policy, personal, and bank account information. The company says the incident is limited to Japan and its U.S. business systems were not accessed.

Aflac disclosed a data breach after attackers accessed systems belonging to its Japan subsidiary, Aflac Life Insurance Japan Ltd. The company said Aflac Japan discovered on June 25, 2026, that an unauthorized third party had unlawfully accessed certain systems between June 15 and June 25, 2026. Aflac is a Fortune 500 company and the largest supplemental insurance provider in the United States, serving millions of customers in the U.S. and Japan.

Aflac Japan took steps intended to contain the incident and prevent further intrusion, including suspending certain systems. The company said it continues to serve policyholders while responding to the incident. Aflac is investigating with external cybersecurity experts and has notified the Japan Financial Services Agency and other relevant authorities.

Although the investigation is ongoing, Aflac Japan determined that some impacted files contain policy and coverage details, personal information, and bank account information. The company said it intends to provide appropriate notifications to individuals affected by the incident. Aflac also said the incident is limited to systems in Japan and that systems related to its U.S. business were not accessed.

  • Aflac has disclosed two data breaches in roughly one year, with the earlier incident occurring during a broader campaign that targeted U.S. insurance companies.
  • The company contained the Japan incident by suspending certain systems while still continuing service to policyholders, indicating that business operations were maintained despite the response measures.
  • Aflac has already determined that bank account information was present in impacted files even though the overall scope and ultimate impact of the incident remain unknown.
  • The article links last year’s Aflac breach to a wider insurance-sector wave associated with Scattered Spider indicators, but it does not attribute the newly disclosed Japan breach to any threat group.
Key Points
  • 01Aflac disclosed a data breach after attackers accessed systems belonging to its Japan subsidiary, Aflac Life Insurance Japan Ltd.
  • 02Aflac Japan discovered on June 25, 2026, that an unauthorized third party had accessed certain systems between June 15 and June 25, 2026.
  • 03Aflac Japan said some impacted files contain policy and coverage details, personal information, and bank account information.
  • 04Aflac Japan suspended certain systems to contain the incident but said it continues to serve policyholders while responding.
  • 05Aflac said the incident is limited to systems in Japan and that systems related to its U.S. business were not accessed.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific, recent data breach at a major insurance company involving personal, policy, and bank account information, making it broadly relevant for security professionals. The full scope is still unknown, and the incident is limited to Aflac Japan, so it is important but not at the highest urgency level.

14news
Fake Perplexity extension on Chrome Web Store tracked searches
bleepingcomputer.com · 30 Jun 2026 15:46 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSchrome-settings-overrideschrome-web-storechromiumdeclarative-net-requestflkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcdgoogle-chromemicrosoft-threat-intelligenceomniboxperplexity-aiperplexity-ai-onlinesearch-for-perplexity-ai
80
In plain words

A fake Chrome browser add-on pretended to be Perplexity AI and sent people’s search queries and search suggestions through the operator’s own systems before sending them on to real search services. People affected are those who installed the Chrome extension named “Search for perplexity ai,” especially the one with ID flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd. Microsoft said it did not find evidence that passwords were stolen, but the add-on had enough access that it could have collected more sensitive information if its operator chose to. Users should remove it and consider changing important passwords as a precaution.

Summary
A fake Chrome extension impersonating Perplexity AI redirected users’ search queries through attacker-controlled infrastructure and collected browsing/search data. Microsoft says users who installed extension ID flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd should remove it and consider rotating critical passwords.

A malicious Chrome Web Store extension called “Search for perplexity ai” masqueraded as the Perplexity AI answer engine. Microsoft Threat Intelligence researchers said the extension intercepted search traffic and collected browsing information. It routed search queries and real-time suggestions through its own infrastructure before redirecting users to legitimate search services.

The fake extension used similar branding to Perplexity AI and the domain “perplexity-ai[.]online,” instead of the legitimate perplexity.ai domain. Once installed, it changed the browser’s search settings to replace the default search provider. Microsoft said it also intercepted and redirected address-bar queries in a Chromium browser’s Omnibox to intermediary infrastructure not associated with the official vendor domain.

Microsoft said it found no evidence that the extension stole credentials or other sensitive information. However, the researchers said its permissions would allow the operator to expand the scope of data theft, and that its DNR permissions enabled traffic redirection, URL rewriting, and selective request filtering. Users who installed the extension with the ID “flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd” should remove it and rotate critical account passwords out of an abundance of caution.

  • The fake extension used a lookalike domain, “perplexity-ai[.]online,” while the legitimate Perplexity domain is “perplexity.ai,” making the domain difference a concrete warning sign for users.
  • Microsoft said logging code found on the extension’s server showed the search-data collection was intentionally designed rather than accidental.
  • The extension’s requested permissions for traffic redirection, URL rewriting, and request filtering were not consistent with expected AI assistant behavior, making its permission set a notable red flag.
  • Although Microsoft found no credential theft, the extension’s permissions were broad enough that the operator could have expanded collection to more sensitive data.
Key Points
  • 01A malicious Chrome Web Store extension called “Search for perplexity ai” masqueraded as the Perplexity AI answer engine.
  • 02Microsoft Threat Intelligence said the extension routed search queries and real-time suggestions through its own infrastructure before redirecting users to legitimate search services.
  • 03The extension changed browser search settings to replace the default search provider and intercept address-bar queries in Chromium browsers.
  • 04Microsoft found no evidence that the extension stole credentials or other sensitive information, but said its permissions would allow the operator to expand the scope of data theft.
  • 05Users who installed the extension with the ID “flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd” should remove it and rotate critical account passwords out of caution.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific malicious Chrome Web Store extension impersonating Perplexity AI and intercepting users’ search traffic, which is broadly relevant because browser extensions can reach sensitive browsing data. The immediate impact appears limited because Microsoft found no credential theft, but affected users are advised to remove the extension and rotate important passwords.

Reports
  • PRIMARYChromium extension uses AI-related branding redirect browser searchresearch
    Microsoft
    The article cites Microsoft’s analysis explaining that the fake extension overrode Chromium browser search settings and redirected Omnibox queries through intermediary infrastructure.
15announcement
Shipping post-quantum cryptography to Python
blog.trailofbits.com · 30 Jun 2026 11:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSansibleapache-airflowaws-lccertbotml-dsaml-kemnistparamikopyca-cryptographypypipythonslh-dsasovereign-tech-agencyunited-stateswhite-house
78
In plain words

The article says the widely used Python cryptography package now includes new tools meant to protect data from future powerful quantum computers, thanks to work funded by the Sovereign Tech Agency. This matters because many Python projects, including Ansible, Certbot, Apache Airflow, and paramiko, depend on this package, so making these protections available there helps the wider Python software world begin moving. The broader stakes are that governments and software makers are preparing for a time when attackers may be able to break today’s common protections, and the U.S. government has already set deadlines for federal systems to use these newer methods.

Summary
Python’s widely used pyca/cryptography library now supports the NIST post-quantum ML-KEM and ML-DSA primitives in version 48, making them available via pip install. This is a major step for Python’s post-quantum migration, but protocols and applications still need integration work because the new keys, signatures, and ciphertexts are much larger than classical equivalents.

Post-quantum cryptography is now available to the Python ecosystem through pyca/cryptography version 48. With funding from the Sovereign Tech Agency, support was implemented for ML-KEM, the NIST-standard key-establishment primitive, and ML-DSA, the NIST-standard digital-signature primitive. The release includes Rust bindings, a cross-binding API and tests, and support for AWS-LC as a cryptographic backend, along with work from maintainers to support other backends.

The article says this matters because almost every Python program that touches cryptography goes through pyca/cryptography, which is currently the eleventh most-downloaded package on PyPI. Projects such as Ansible, Certbot, Apache Airflow, and paramiko depend on it, so the Python ecosystem could not begin migrating if the library did not expose post-quantum algorithms. The article also cites a White House order requiring high-value and high-impact U.S. federal systems to use post-quantum key establishment by December 31, 2030, and post-quantum digital signatures by December 31, 2031.

The article emphasizes that this is not a drop-in replacement for existing cryptography because post-quantum public keys, signatures, and ciphertexts are much larger than classical equivalents. It compares Ed25519 with ML-DSA-65 and X25519 with ML-KEM-768, showing substantially larger public keys, signatures, and ciphertexts for the post-quantum options. SLH-DSA is not supported in pyca/cryptography 48, but work has started, and broader protocol integration is still needed before tools such as Certbot or Ansible are likely to use these algorithms directly.

  • A post-quantum migration in Python cannot be treated as a simple algorithm swap because existing protocols or wire formats that assume 32-byte Ed25519 or X25519 values may need larger fields, length prefixes, and chunking behavior.
  • ML-KEM changes integration patterns because it is a key encapsulation mechanism rather than a Diffie-Hellman-style exchange, so applications must use encapsulation and decapsulation instead of both parties combining key shares.
  • The article frames pyca/cryptography support as a prerequisite rather than a complete migration, because tools such as Certbot or Ansible are unlikely to use these algorithms directly until common protocols add support.
  • SLH-DSA is positioned as a conservative fallback to lattice-based signatures because it relies only on hash-function security properties, but its large signatures and slow signing make its tradeoffs different from ML-DSA.
Key Points
  • 01The pyca/cryptography package added support for the NIST-standard post-quantum primitives ML-KEM for key establishment and ML-DSA for digital signatures in cryptography version 48.
  • 02The work was funded by the Sovereign Tech Agency and includes Rust bindings, a cross-binding API and tests, and support for AWS-LC as a cryptographic backend.
  • 03The article says pyca/cryptography is widely used across the Python ecosystem, including by projects such as Ansible, Certbot, Apache Airflow, and paramiko.
  • 04Post-quantum primitives have larger public keys, signatures, and ciphertexts than classical algorithms such as Ed25519 and X25519, so migration may require changes to protocols, wire formats, length fields, and chunking assumptions.
  • 05SLH-DSA is not supported in pyca/cryptography 48, but work on it has started, and broader protocol integration is still needed before post-quantum migration is complete.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific release affecting a widely used Python cryptography library and has broad relevance for security professionals tracking post-quantum migration. It is not an urgent incident or patch, but it materially changes what the Python ecosystem can implement today.

Reports
  • PRIMARYFIPS 204: Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Standardresearch
    NIST
    The article links FIPS 204 as the standard defining ML-DSA, the post-quantum digital-signature primitive added to pyca/cryptography.
  • PRIMARYFIPS 203: Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism Standardresearch
    NIST
    The article links FIPS 203 as the standard defining ML-KEM, the post-quantum key-encapsulation primitive added to pyca/cryptography.
  • FIPS 205: Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Standardresearch
    NIST
    The article links FIPS 205 when discussing SLH-DSA, a NIST hash-based digital signature standard that is not yet supported in pyca/cryptography 48.
16news
Full Authentication Pass, Zero Legitimacy: How a 26-Day-Old Domain Ran ACH Fraud
ironscales.com · 30 Jun 2026 11:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSach-fraudcat-hphishdkimdmarcironscalesmiamimiaminternationalyachtsales-commicrosoftsendgridspft1534t1566-001themisunited-statesutah
78
In plain words

Attackers created a new website address that looked like a Miami yacht sales business and, 25 days later, sent an email to an information-services company saying an ACH payment had been completed. The email looked technically valid and the attached PDF had no harmful links, but the business identity did not check out and the listed phone number pointed to Utah instead of Miami. This matters because finance teams can be tricked into acting quickly on fake payment messages, and these scams are among the most financially damaging types of online crime.

Summary
A newly registered fake vendor domain used SendGrid to pass SPF, DKIM, and DMARC while sending an ACH payment confirmation lure with a clean PDF, showing that authentication does not prove legitimacy. Financial emails from new domains should trigger mandatory out-of-band verification even when all email security checks pass.

Attackers registered miaminternationalyachtsales[.]com on February 8, 2026, and used it 25 days later to send an “ACH Payment Completed” email to an information-services company. The message claimed to confirm a completed ACH payment and included a PDF attachment with supposed payment details. The PDF contained no malicious links, embedded scripts, or macro content, making the threat entirely social engineering-based.

The domain passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication checks because the email was delivered through SendGrid, a legitimate email delivery platform. The article emphasizes that authentication confirms authorized sending for a domain but does not prove the domain belongs to a real business, that the claimed transaction occurred, or that the business identity is verifiable. The claimed Miami, Florida business identity was also inconsistent with a phone number in the email body that geolocated to Utah.

Microsoft classified the message as CAT:HPHISH, a high-confidence phishing designation, despite the clean authentication results. IRONSCALES Adaptive AI scored the message at 51% confidence, which the article presents as a borderline result compared with Microsoft’s stronger classification. The article argues that newly registered domains sending financial-themed emails such as ACH confirmations, invoices, or payment requests should trigger mandatory out-of-band verification regardless of authentication status.

  • A full SPF, DKIM, and DMARC pass can create a misleading sense of trust because it only confirms authorized use of the sending domain, not whether the domain represents a real vendor or a legitimate transaction.
  • Clean attachments can still support payment fraud by making a fake transaction notice look more credible without giving automated scanners malicious links or code to detect.
  • The article treats domain age as an actionable control point, recommending mandatory out-of-band verification for ACH, invoice, or payment emails from domains registered within the past 90 days.
  • The difference between IRONSCALES’ 51% confidence score and Microsoft’s CAT:HPHISH classification shows that borderline results from one detection system should be weighed against stronger independent signals from another.
  • Geographic inconsistencies in business details, such as a Miami-branded company using a Utah-geolocated phone number, are quick verification clues that can expose fake-vendor payment scams.
Key Points
  • 01Attackers registered miaminternationalyachtsales.com on February 8, 2026 and sent an “ACH Payment Completed” email with a PDF attachment on March 5, 2026, 25 days later.
  • 02The email was delivered through SendGrid and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication checks, but the article states that these checks do not verify whether the domain belongs to a legitimate business.
  • 03The PDF attachment contained no malicious links, embedded scripts, or macro content, making the threat entirely based on social engineering around a supposed completed ACH payment.
  • 04The claimed Miami, Florida business identity conflicted with a phone number in the email body that geolocated to Utah.
  • 05Microsoft classified the message as CAT:HPHISH, or high-confidence phishing, while IRONSCALES Adaptive AI scored it at 51% confidence.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

The article reports a concrete BEC/payment fraud case involving a newly registered domain, SendGrid delivery, full SPF/DKIM/DMARC pass, and Microsoft high-confidence phishing classification. It is broadly relevant for security professionals because it shows a practical authentication bypass of trust assumptions in financial email workflows, though it is not a major breach, critical patch, or named threat actor operation.

Reports
  • 2026 Data Breach Investigations Reportresearch
    Verizon
    The article cites the Verizon DBIR 2026 as identifying BEC and pretexting as the fastest-growing financial fraud vectors in the enterprise.
  • Spearphishing Attachment, T1566.001research
    MITRE ATT&CK
    The article references MITRE ATT&CK to classify attachment-based spearphishing delivery as T1566.001.
  • Recognize and Report Phishingguide
    CISA
    The article cites CISA guidance advising finance teams to use callback verification for ACH or wire transfer instructions received by email.
17announcement
Microsoft accelerates quantum-safe roadmap as risks grow
bleepingcomputer.com · 30 Jun 2026 21:20 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSapplechromegoogleharvest-now-decrypt-laterimessagemicrosoftmicrosoft-quantum-safe-programsecure-future-initiativesignaltls-1-3
78
In plain words

Microsoft says it is speeding up plans to protect its important products and services from future quantum computers, aiming to make the switch by 2029. This matters because stolen encrypted information from today could be saved and unlocked later if future machines become powerful enough. Companies and organizations that rely on Microsoft services, and anyone whose sensitive data they hold, could be affected if protections are not updated in time.

Summary
Microsoft is accelerating its move to post-quantum cryptography, aiming to transition critical products and services by 2029 as the risk of future quantum computers breaking today’s encryption grows. The company says organizations should start modernizing cryptography now to prepare for “harvest now, decrypt later” threats.

Microsoft announced that it is accelerating its quantum-safe security roadmap, saying advances in quantum computing are making the need to replace today’s encryption standards more urgent. The company said current quantum computers cannot crack modern encryption, but warned about “harvest now, decrypt later” attacks, where encrypted data stolen today is stored until future quantum computers can decrypt it. Microsoft now plans to transition critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029 through its Microsoft Quantum Safe Program.

Microsoft said the risk horizon has shifted and that cryptographically relevant quantum computers could arrive sooner than previously expected. The company is also adding quantum-safe requirements into its Secure Future Initiative so readiness can be tracked alongside other security goals. Other companies, including Apple, Google, and Signal, have also begun integrating post-quantum cryptography to replace existing public-key encryption algorithms with quantum-resistant versions.

Microsoft outlined three priorities for the transition: upgrading network cryptography with modern protocols such as TLS 1.3, building crypto-agility so algorithms can be swapped without redesigning applications, and modernizing cryptographic trust chains for code signing, certificate issuance, software updates, and hardware-backed key protection. The company said organizations should begin preparing now because the transition will be significant in scale and complexity. Microsoft has not publicly detailed which specific advances prompted the accelerated roadmap or why it believes quantum computers could arrive sooner than previously expected.

  • Microsoft is framing quantum-safe readiness as a broader infrastructure modernization effort, not just a one-time replacement of encryption algorithms.
  • The article highlights TLS 1.3 adoption as a near-term step organizations can take because it can support future hybrid and post-quantum key exchange.
  • By adding quantum-safe requirements to the Secure Future Initiative, Microsoft plans to track post-quantum readiness alongside its other security goals rather than treating it as a separate effort.
  • Microsoft’s accelerated timeline is notable because the company did not publicly identify the specific quantum computing advances that changed its risk assessment.
Key Points
  • 01Microsoft announced that it is accelerating its quantum-safe security roadmap because advances in quantum computing are making the replacement of today’s encryption standards more urgent.
  • 02Microsoft plans to transition critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029 through its Microsoft Quantum Safe Program.
  • 03The company warned that “harvest now, decrypt later” attacks could expose sensitive encrypted data stolen today once future quantum computers become powerful enough to decrypt it.
  • 04Microsoft said organizations should upgrade network cryptography, build crypto-agility, and modernize cryptographic trust chains to support the transition.
  • 05Microsoft has not publicly detailed which specific quantum computing advances prompted the accelerated roadmap.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

Microsoft’s accelerated post-quantum cryptography roadmap is a specific vendor announcement with broad relevance for security planning, especially for organizations relying on Microsoft products and services. It is not an immediate incident or critical patch, but it signals a concrete timeline and priorities for a major security transition.

18research
What the Numbers Say About FIFA 2026 Cyber Risk
thehackernews.com · 30 Jun 2026 11:30 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScheck-point-researchdmarcdomain-spoofingemail-impersonationfake-mobile-appsfifafifa-world-cup-2026godaddygoogle-playhostingernamecheapphishingproofpointtelegramtop-tld
78
In plain words

The article says criminals had already prepared scams before the FIFA World Cup 2026 began, including fake emails, fake betting apps, and fake hotel or travel websites. Fans, hotels, airlines, betting companies, sponsors, and other event partners could be affected because people may be tricked into sending money, sharing account details, or trusting messages that look real. The broader risk is that the huge rush around a global event makes it easier for scams to spread before people have time to check carefully.

Summary
Check Point says cybercriminals had already staged World Cup 2026 fraud infrastructure before kickoff, including spoofable partner domains, fake sportsbook apps, Telegram scams, and phishing travel sites. Organizations in travel, hospitality, finance, and gambling should treat the tournament period as high risk for impersonation and fraud.

Check Point Research said fraud infrastructure targeting the FIFA World Cup 2026 had already been built, staged, and partially deployed by the June 11 opening date. Check Point Exposure Management published the FIFA World Cup 2026 Cyber Threat Report covering financial services, transportation, hospitality, and gambling. The article highlights pre-planned threat actor activity across three sectors and at least ten languages.

Proofpoint found that more than one-third of official FIFA World Cup 2026 partners lacked sufficient DMARC enforcement to prevent domain spoofing. The article says this could allow attackers to send emails appearing to come from sponsors, vendors, or logistics partners without a technical barrier stopping them. It also notes that the World Cup supply chain includes airlines, hotels, broadcast partners, merchandise contractors, and catering companies, where high transaction volumes and tight deadlines can reduce payment verification rigor.

The article says fake sportsbook apps surged sharply before the tournament, with 64 impersonator app detections in the 2026 pre-tournament window compared with zero in the 2025 baseline, concentrated in April and May 2026 and on Google Play. Check Point Exposure Management also tracked FIFA-themed lookalike domains targeting travel and hospitality services, with April 2026 accounting for 21.9% of the 12-month sample and March plus April representing 34%. Some fraudulent domains had MX records configured, meaning they could receive email, support reply-path impersonation, and intercept password-reset flows from victim accounts.

  • The sportsbook app activity was not isolated brand abuse, because at least five developer accounts published apps spoofing two or more different sportsbook brands within hours or days of each other.
  • The travel and hospitality phishing infrastructure was concentrated enough to support focused takedown efforts, with GoDaddy, Hostinger, Namecheap, Porkbun, and IONOS together hosting 56% of the fraudulent domains.
  • The .top domain ending was disproportionately represented, accounting for 28% of registrations and described in the article as favored for phishing because of low abuse-response thresholds and cheap registration costs.
  • Some fake travel and hotel domains were more than static scam pages because their MX records allowed them to receive email and support reply-path impersonation or password-reset interception.
  • The Telegram-based fake tipster scheme kept victims engaged by splitting betting picks across the audience so that roughly half of subscribers would appear to win enough to keep depositing.
Key Points
  • 01Check Point Research said fraud infrastructure targeting the FIFA World Cup 2026 had already been built, staged, and partially deployed by the June 11 opening date.
  • 02Proofpoint found that more than one-third of official FIFA World Cup 2026 partners lacked sufficient DMARC enforcement to prevent domain spoofing.
  • 03A comparison of eight major sportsbook brands found zero impersonator app detections in a 2025 baseline window and 64 detections in the pre-tournament 2026 window, concentrated in April and May 2026 and on Google Play.
  • 04Check Point Exposure Management tracked FIFA-themed lookalike domains targeting travel and hospitality services from November 2025 through May 2026, with April 2026 accounting for 21.9% of the 12-month sample.
  • 05A subset of the fraudulent travel and hospitality domains had MX records configured, meaning they could receive email, run reply-path impersonation, and intercept password-reset flows from victim accounts.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

The article reports specific pre-tournament findings about staged fraud infrastructure targeting FIFA World Cup 2026, including spoofable partner domains, fake sportsbook apps, Telegram scams, and lookalike travel sites. It has broad relevance for security and fraud teams in travel, hospitality, finance, and gambling, though it is based on vendor research rather than a single major breach or critical exploit.

Reports
  • PRIMARYFIFA World Cup 2026 Cyber Threat Reportresearch
    Check Point Exposure Management
    The article says Check Point Exposure Management published this report covering cyber threats to financial services, transportation, hospitality, and gambling around FIFA World Cup 2026, and the article summarizes several of its findings.
19opinion
How CISA BOD 26-04 redefines vulnerability management metrics for security leaders
tenable.com · 30 Jun 2026 13:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScisacisa-bod-26-04cyentia-institutefederal-civilian-executive-branchknown-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalogtenabletenable-oneunited-statesvulnrichmentxm-cyber
78
In plain words

A new CISA directive changes how U.S. federal agencies must decide which software security problems to fix first, requiring them to prove why they fixed some quickly and delayed others. This affects federal agencies directly and may also affect thousands of federal contractors through contract requirements. The article says this matters because leaders and boards are being asked to show that they are reducing real-world risk, not just reporting how many fixes they completed.

Summary
CISA BOD 26-04 shifts vulnerability management from counting patches to proving risk-based prioritization, requiring agencies and contractors to document why vulnerabilities are remediated or deferred. Security leaders should focus reporting on attack-surface visibility and remediation of the highest-risk tiers rather than traditional metrics like total patches or average patch time.

CISA BOD 26-04 changes how federal agencies patch vulnerabilities and how security leaders must measure, justify, and communicate cyber risk. The article says the directive requires agencies to make and defend risk-based prioritization decisions, including decisions to defer remediation. This turns vulnerability management from a technical operation into a governance discipline that requires audit-ready documentation.

The article argues that traditional metrics such as total vulnerabilities patched, mean time to patch, and percentage of systems scanned are insufficient under BOD 26-04. It says the more important measures are coverage breadth, remediation compliance by BOD tier, coverage of actively exploited vulnerabilities, exposure surface reduction, forensic triage completion, deferral justification, and time from KEV addition to remediation. Tenable’s analysis of customer telemetry is described as showing that monitoring coverage breadth is a stronger predictor of risk posture than patch speed, and the article cites independent research showing organizations can remediate only about 10% of open vulnerabilities per month.

Although BOD 26-04 formally applies to Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies, the article says its reach extends to federal contractors through contract compliance requirements. It also frames the directive as part of a broader shift in insurance underwriting, board accountability, and sector regulation toward proving reduction of actual risk rather than reporting activity volume. The article recommends that security leaders audit current reporting, establish a four-variable data foundation, build an audit trail, and communicate to boards using monitoring coverage breadth and risk-tier remediation rate.

  • Reducing public exposure may be one of the highest-leverage compliance actions because Tenable’s analysis found that removing an asset from public exposure can shift 76.7% of its associated CVEs to longer remediation windows.
  • A strong mean-time-to-remediate metric can hide serious risk because remediation half-lives differ widely by asset type, from 36 days for Microsoft Windows systems to 369 days for network appliances.
  • The article highlights that many patched vulnerabilities may not materially reduce risk, citing research that 75% of security exposures do not put critical assets at risk and only 2% sit on attack-path choke points.
  • BOD 26-04 makes deferral an auditable governance decision, meaning dashboards and reports may function as compliance evidence rather than simply operational status updates.
  • The article recommends a simple board-level model using monitoring coverage breadth as a leading indicator and risk-tier remediation rate as a trailing indicator, rather than overwhelming executives with detailed vulnerability counts.
Key Points
  • 01CISA BOD 26-04 requires federal agencies to make and defend risk-based vulnerability prioritization decisions, including documented justifications when remediation is deferred.
  • 02The article argues that traditional vulnerability management metrics such as total vulnerabilities patched, mean time to remediate, and percentage of systems scanned do not measure what BOD 26-04 requires.
  • 03Tenable’s analysis of customer telemetry found that monitoring coverage breadth is a stronger predictor of risk posture than patch speed, and the article cites independent research showing organizations can remediate only about 10% of open vulnerabilities per month.
  • 04BOD 26-04’s operational reach extends beyond Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies because CISA requires agencies to review contracts for modifications needed to comply with the directive.
  • 05The article recommends metrics such as remediation compliance by BOD tier, coverage of actively exploited vulnerabilities, exposure surface reduction over time, forensic triage completion rate, deferral justification documentation rate, and mean time from KEV addition to remediation.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

The article centers on a specific CISA directive with broad implications for federal agencies and federal contractors, especially around vulnerability prioritization, reporting, and audit documentation. It is not an urgent exploitation or patching event, but it is a concrete policy change that many security leaders may need to operationalize.

Reports
  • PRIMARYCISA BOD 26-04: Prioritizing security updates based on riskadvisory
    CISA
    The article is built around CISA’s BOD 26-04 directive and explains how it changes vulnerability prioritization, remediation timelines, documentation, and reporting expectations.
20announcement
Microsoft adds smarter bot protection to Teams meetings
bleepingcomputer.com · 30 Jun 2026 10:52 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSblack-bastabrand-impersonationcross-tenant-chatshelpdesk-impersonationmatanbuchusmicrosoftmicrosoft-365microsoft-defendermicrosoft-teamssocial-engineeringteams-admin-center
70
  • └
    announcement
    Microsoft adds smarter bot protection to Teams meetings
    bleepingcomputer.com · 30 Jun 2026 10:52 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
    TAGSandroidblack-bastahelpdesk-impersonationiosmacosmatanbuchusmicrosoftmicrosoft-defendermicrosoft-teamssocial-engineeringteams-admin-centerwindows
    In plain words

    Microsoft has added a new Teams setting that lets meeting organizers stop outside automated accounts from joining meetings unless they approve them first. This affects organizations that use Microsoft Teams, including people who organize or attend meetings where note-taking tools or other automated helpers may try to join. It matters because criminals have been using Teams to trick employees, pretend to be IT or helpdesk staff, and push harmful software or gain access to company networks.

    Summary
    Microsoft has added a Teams admin policy that detects third-party or suspicious bots, holds them in the meeting lobby, labels them, and requires organizer approval before they can join. The feature is meant to reduce abuse of Teams by malicious apps and social-engineering attackers while giving organizations more control over external bots.

    Microsoft introduced a new Teams admin policy that allows organizers to prevent third-party bots from joining meetings without approval. The feature was first announced in a March Microsoft 365 roadmap entry and is available across Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS for worldwide standard multi-tenant and GCC cloud environments. Microsoft says the policy gives organizations more control and visibility over external bots in meetings.

    When enabled, Teams detects potential bots, places them in the meeting lobby, clearly identifies them, and prompts organizers to confirm admission. This applies even when meeting settings allow participants to bypass the lobby, because bots identified through the policy still require approval before joining. The policy, called “Manage external bots and their access to meetings,” can be assigned to individual users or specific groups in the Teams Admin Center.

    Microsoft is also planning additional controls, including allow lists for approved bots, policies to block external bots entirely, admin reports, audit logs, and more granular settings for different security requirements. The article notes that attackers have abused Teams for social engineering, including cybercrime gangs and ransomware groups using the platform to target employees. Microsoft has also added or planned other Teams protections, including external user blocking through the Defender portal, fraud-protection warnings for calls, and suspicious call reporting.

    • The bot approval requirement still applies even in meetings where organizers allow participants to bypass the lobby, so the control is not weakened by more permissive lobby settings.
    • The policy can be applied to individual users or specific groups, allowing organizations to focus bot controls on higher-risk teams or meeting organizers instead of enabling it uniformly for everyone.
    • Microsoft is treating external Teams abuse as broader than meeting bots, with separate controls for blocking external users through the Defender portal and warnings for impersonation in calls.
    • The planned allow lists, full blocking policies, reports, and audit logs indicate that Microsoft expects organizations to need both prevention and visibility into bot activity in Teams meetings.
    Key Points
    • 01Microsoft introduced a new Teams admin policy that allows organizers to prevent third-party bots from joining meetings without approval.
    • 02When the policy is enabled, Teams detects potential bots, places them in the meeting lobby, identifies them, and prompts organizers to confirm admission.
    • 03The policy can be assigned to individual users or specific groups through the Teams Admin Center.
    • 04Microsoft plans to add more admin controls, including allow lists for approved bots, policies to block external bots entirely, admin reports, audit logs, and more granular controls.
    • 05Microsoft said attackers are increasingly abusing external Teams collaboration by impersonating IT or helpdesk staff to contact employees and trick them into granting remote access.
    OPEN SOURCE ↗
    JUSTIFICATION

    This reports a specific Microsoft Teams security control with broad relevance for organizations that use Teams and need to manage third-party bots in meetings. It is not an urgent incident or critical patch, but it is actionable for security professionals responsible for collaboration-platform abuse and social-engineering risks.

In plain words

Microsoft has added a new Teams setting that lets organizations stop outside automated meeting tools from joining meetings unless the organizer approves them. This affects companies and other groups that use Teams, especially meeting organizers and attendees who may not realize a non-human participant has been added. It matters because criminals have been abusing Teams to contact employees, pretend to be trusted support staff, and trick people into giving access or information, so clearer warnings and approval steps can help keep unwanted participants out.

Summary
Microsoft introduced a Teams admin policy that detects suspected third-party bots, holds them in the meeting lobby, labels them, and requires organizer approval before they can join. The feature is meant to reduce malicious bot abuse and improve visibility and control over non-human participants in Teams meetings.

Microsoft has introduced a new Teams admin policy that allows organizers to prevent third-party bots from joining meetings without approval. The feature was first announced in a March Microsoft 365 roadmap entry and is intended for Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS across worldwide standard multi-tenant and GCC cloud environments. Microsoft says the policy gives organizations more control and visibility over external bots in meetings and helps ensure only intended participants and tools can join.

When enabled, Teams automatically detects potential bots, places them in the meeting lobby, clearly identifies them, and prompts organizers to approve admission. The policy, called Manage external bots and their access to meetings, can be assigned in the Teams Admin Center to individual users or specific groups. Microsoft said suspected bots will still require approval even in meetings where organizers allow participants to bypass the lobby.

Microsoft also plans to add more controls, including allow lists for approved bots, policies to block external bots entirely, admin reports, audit logs, and more granular controls for different security requirements. The article notes that attackers have abused Teams for social engineering, including external collaboration, cross-tenant chats, helpdesk impersonation, and attempts to trick employees into granting remote access or exposing data. Microsoft has also added or planned related Teams protections, including external user blocking through the Defender portal, fraud-protection warnings for calls, and suspicious call reporting.

  • The new policy still forces suspected bots into the lobby even when a meeting is configured to let participants bypass the lobby, which makes bot approval a separate safeguard from normal attendee admission settings.
  • Organizations can apply the bot access policy to specific users or groups rather than enabling it tenant-wide, allowing different protections for different meeting risk levels.
  • Microsoft is treating Teams meeting abuse as part of a broader security problem, with related controls planned or added for external users, suspicious calls, impersonation warnings, reporting, audit logs, and bot allow lists.
  • The planned allow lists and full external-bot blocking options indicate Microsoft expects organizations to need both approved automation for legitimate tools and stricter controls for higher-security meetings.
Key Points
  • 01Microsoft introduced a new Teams admin policy that lets meeting organizers prevent third-party bots from joining meetings without approval.
  • 02When enabled, Teams detects potential bots, places them in the meeting lobby, identifies them, and prompts organizers to confirm whether they should be admitted.
  • 03The policy can be assigned in the Teams Admin Center to individual users or specific groups.
  • 04Microsoft plans to add more admin controls, including allow lists for approved bots, policies to block external bots entirely, admin reports, audit logs, and more granular security controls.
  • 05Microsoft said attackers are increasingly abusing external Teams collaboration by impersonating IT or helpdesk staff to contact employees and trick them into granting remote access or sharing data.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific Microsoft Teams security feature rollout with practical relevance for organizations managing meeting access and external bots. It is not an urgent incident, active exploitation report, or critical patch, but it is actionable for security teams responsible for Teams administration and social engineering defenses.

May Readⓘ 4 items
01news
House passes kids’ online safety bill, but Senate approval unlikely
therecord.media · 30 Jun 2026 16:23 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGScaliforniahouse-energy-and-commerce-committeehouse-of-representativeskids-actkids-online-safety-actlori-trahanmarsha-blackburnparentsosrichard-blumenthalsam-liccardosenateunited-stateswhite-house
70
In plain words

The House passed a bill meant to make the internet safer for children, including rules about AI chatbots, porn age checks, data brokers, ads aimed at minors, and features that can encourage addiction or unsafe contact. But the bill is unlikely to pass the Senate because key senators say it is too weak and does not force big tech companies to put children’s safety first when designing their platforms. Families, children, internet users, tech companies, states, and lawmakers are affected, with the broader fight centered on how to protect kids online without creating new privacy risks for everyone.

Summary
The House passed the KIDS Act to add online safety rules for children, but the bill is unlikely to advance in the Senate because key senators and advocates say it lacks stronger protections like a duty of care for tech platforms. The measure also faces criticism over privacy concerns tied to age verification requirements.

The House passed the Kids Internet and Digital Safety (KIDS) Act on Monday night by a 267-117 vote, meeting the two-thirds majority required under an expedited voting process. The bill is intended to bolster children’s online safety, but it has been criticized by many advocates and key senators as privacy invasive and too weak. Despite its bipartisan support in the House, the bill faces a steep climb in the Senate, where many lawmakers support a competing measure with stronger safeguards.

A central dispute is that the House bill does not include a duty of care provision, which would force big tech companies to put children’s safety first in their platform designs. Sens. Richard Blumenthal and Marsha Blackburn, authors of the bipartisan Kids Online Safety Act, criticized the House bill as a “pale imitation of Big Tech accountability” and pledged to block it. The House bill also does not block more restrictive state laws and leaves out language preempting some state artificial intelligence laws, an issue that divides Republicans and Democrats.

The KIDS Act would require AI chatbots to disclose they are not human, require age verification for users seeking to view porn, and impose requirements on data brokers handling children’s information. Digital freedoms and civil liberties advocates condemned the age verification element as a threat to the privacy of all internet users. ParentsSOS said the bill lacks a duty of care but praised provisions addressing stronger state regulations, addictive features, geolocation sharing, outreach from unknown adults, targeted ads to minors, and a standard preventing big tech from claiming it does not know preteens are on their platforms.

  • The bill’s strong bipartisan House vote does not indicate likely enactment because Senate opposition is centered on a specific missing safeguard, the duty of care provision, rather than general opposition to children’s online safety legislation.
  • State-law preemption is a major dividing line in the negotiations, with the House compromise preserving states’ ability to pass stricter kids’ online safety laws while many Republicans and the administration favor preempting some state artificial intelligence laws.
  • Support and opposition do not break neatly along party lines, as some Democrats backed the imperfect House bill while a bipartisan Senate pair, Blumenthal and Blackburn, pledged to block it.
  • The KIDS Act is being criticized from opposite directions: some advocates say it is too weak on Big Tech accountability, while digital freedoms and civil liberties groups say its age verification requirement threatens privacy for all internet users.
Key Points
  • 01The House passed the Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act on Monday night by a 267-117 vote, meeting the two-thirds majority required under the expedited voting process used.
  • 02The KIDS Act faces a difficult path in the Senate, where many lawmakers support a competing Kids Online Safety Act that includes a duty of care provision requiring big tech to prioritize children’s safety in platform design.
  • 03Sens. Richard Blumenthal and Marsha Blackburn criticized the House bill as a weak imitation of Big Tech accountability and pledged to block it because it does not include a duty of care.
  • 04The House bill would require AI chatbots to disclose they are not human, require age verification for users seeking to view porn, and impose requirements on data brokers handling children’s information.
  • 05Digital freedoms and civil liberties advocates condemned the bill, arguing that its age verification element threatens the privacy of all internet users.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific legislative development with potential privacy, age-verification, data broker, and platform-safety implications, but it is not an active threat, breach, vulnerability, or patch event. Security and privacy professionals may want to track it for regulatory impact, though the article says Senate approval is unlikely.

02news
An intelligence budget 'super user' job is now in the hands of Russ Vought
therecord.media · 30 Jun 2026 13:52 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSamaryllis-fox-kennedybill-pulteciadonald-trumpmilitary-intelligence-programnational-intelligence-programnational-security-agencynational-security-councilnistoffice-of-management-and-budgetoffice-of-the-director-of-national-intelligenceproject-2025russ-voughtunited-stateswhite-house
68
In plain words

Russ Vought, President Trump’s budget chief, has personally taken over managing the secret spending plans for major U.S. intelligence agencies like the CIA and NSA after Amaryllis Fox Kennedy left the role. This affects U.S. spy agencies and the White House because the job helps decide how tens of billions of dollars are spent on highly sensitive intelligence work. It matters because the shift gives one senior budget official unusually direct control at a time when the administration is also trying to shrink the intelligence community’s top office.

Summary
OMB Director Russ Vought has personally taken over day-to-day oversight of classified U.S. intelligence budgets after Amaryllis Fox Kennedy’s departure, giving him unusually direct influence over spending for agencies like the CIA and NSA. The move is drawing concern because it comes amid broader Trump administration efforts to shrink and reshape the intelligence community.

Russell Vought, director of the White House Office of Management and Budget, has taken hands-on responsibility for overseeing the classified budgets of major U.S. intelligence agencies. The shift followed the departure of Amaryllis Fox Kennedy, who had held multiple senior roles, including one at OMB. Four people familiar with the matter said it is unclear how long Vought will manage day-to-day budget oversight for agencies such as the CIA and National Security Agency.

The role is significant because it oversees secret spending plans for the intelligence community and requires extensive access to classified programs. For the upcoming fiscal year, the administration has requested almost \(82 billion for the National Intelligence Program and \)50 billion for the Military Intelligence Program. The article says the position works closely with the National Security Council and was powerful enough that some viewed it as a “shadow” national security adviser.

The move comes as the administration works to shrink the intelligence community’s top office and as Trump has named Bill Pulte as director of national intelligence with orders to downsize that office. Sources cited concerns about whether Vought’s involvement reflects broader scrutiny of intelligence spending, including CIA funding practices and quantum computing investments. The article notes that Trump recently signed executive orders to accelerate U.S. quantum technology development and require federal agencies to transition to quantum-resistant cryptography.

  • The intelligence budget position is unusually powerful because it works closely with the National Security Council and has been viewed by some as a “shadow” national security adviser.
  • The role can give its holder a “super user” level of influence over national security spending, including the costs of CIA covert actions around the world.
  • The article says the many required classified program approvals make the position hard to fill quickly, which may help explain why Vought took over directly rather than a new appointee immediately replacing Kennedy.
  • The administration’s quantum computing push could become a major focus of intelligence budget scrutiny because the article says billions of dollars are already involved and the effort may cost tens of billions more.
Key Points
  • 01Russell Vought, director of the White House Office of Management and Budget, has assumed hands-on responsibility for overseeing the classified budgets of U.S. intelligence agencies following Amaryllis Fox Kennedy’s departure.
  • 02The classified spending plans under Vought’s increased scrutiny include requested budgets of almost $82 billion for the National Intelligence Program and $50 billion for the Military Intelligence Program for the upcoming fiscal year.
  • 03Four people familiar with the matter said it is unclear how long Vought will handle day-to-day budget oversight for agencies such as the CIA and the National Security Agency.
