  • 04The article says the intelligence budget role requires extensive security access, specialized expertise, and close work with the National Security Council because it covers highly sensitive programs tied to administration priorities.
  • 05The shift comes as President Donald Trump has named federal mortgage chief Bill Pulte as director of national intelligence with orders to downsize that office.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific personnel and governance shift affecting oversight of classified U.S. intelligence budgets, including CIA, NSA, and quantum-related spending. It is relevant for security professionals tracking national security policy and intelligence funding, but it does not describe an active cyber incident, vulnerability, breach, or technical finding.

03announcement
Kali Linux 2026.2 released with 9 new tools, NetHunter updates
bleepingcomputer.com · 30 Jun 2026 09:46 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSarsenal-nggnomehydra-gtkkali-linuxkali-nethunterkde-plasmalegbamagiskoletoolspenelopeshell-gpttailscaletookie-osinturowifipumpkin3
64
In plain words

Kali Linux 2026.2 has been released with new tools, faster startup for virtual-machine users, updated desktop options, and improvements for its Android phone version, Kali NetHunter. This matters mainly to cybersecurity professionals and ethical hackers who use Kali to check systems, test networks, and do security research. People using Kali on computers, virtual machines, Raspberry Pi devices, Android phones, or Windows Subsystem for Linux may want to upgrade or download the new version.

Summary
Kali Linux 2026.2 is now available with 9 new security tools, a Linux 6.19 kernel, GNOME 50 and KDE Plasma 6.6, plus faster VM boot times. The release also brings major Kali NetHunter improvements, including Magisk kernel flashing support, new phone kernels, and an EvilTwin Wi-Fi fake AP feature.

Kali Linux 2026.2, the second release of the year, is now available for download. The distro is designed for cybersecurity professionals and ethical hackers and includes tools for security audits, penetration testing, and network research. This release adds 9 new tools, numerous Kali NetHunter improvements, updated helper scripts, desktop environment updates, and VM boot tweaks.

The new tools added to the network repositories are arsenal-ng, hydra-gtk, legba, oletools, penelope, shell-gpt, tailscale, tookie-osint, and uro. The Kali Team also updated numerous packages, added new libraries, and upgraded the Kali kernel to 6.19, with the 7.0 kernel available in kali-experimental. Kali Linux now ships with GNOME 50 and KDE Plasma 6.6.

For virtual machine users, pre-built Kali Linux VM images no longer include graphics firmware, reducing the initrd to 60 MB and cutting boot time by about three times in Kali’s QEMU test. Kali NetHunter updates include Magisk standalone kernel flashing support, new kernels with qcacld3 injection support, kernels for more phones, NetHunter Pro bare metal support on more phones, and a new EvilTwin tab with a password verification captive portal. Existing Kali users can upgrade with the provided commands, and successful upgrades can be checked with grep VERSION /etc/os-release.

  • VM users should see the biggest operational benefit from this release because Kali removed graphics firmware from pre-built VM images, shrinking the initrd to 60 MB and cutting boot time by about three times in Kali’s QEMU test.
  • Bare-metal users do not receive the same boot-size optimization automatically, and the Kali Team says they must uninstall unneeded graphics firmware themselves if they want to reduce the 200 MB initrd.
  • Kali users can verify a successful upgrade by running grep VERSION /etc/os-release after completing the documented upgrade commands.
  • NetHunter users can now open newly built kernel installer zip files directly in the Magisk app when the zip was built with the kali-nethunter-installer.
  • The new EvilTwin tab includes a password verification captive portal and an iptables fix intended to make Android Hotspot work properly after using Wifipumpkin3 or EvilTwin.
Key Points
  • 01Kali Linux 2026.2 is available for download with 9 new tools and numerous Kali NetHunter improvements.
  • 02The release adds arsenal-ng, hydra-gtk, legba, oletools, penelope, shell-gpt, tailscale, tookie-osint, and uro to the network repositories.
  • 03Kali Linux 2026.2 upgrades the Kali kernel to 6.19, with the 7.0 kernel available in kali-experimental.
  • 04Pre-built Kali Linux VM images no longer include graphics firmware, reducing the VM initrd to 60 MB and cutting boot time by about three times in the Kali Team’s QEMU test.
  • 05Kali NetHunter updates include Magisk standalone kernel flashing support, new kernels with qcacld3 injection support, more phone kernels, NetHunter Pro bare metal support on more phones, and a new EvilTwin tab with a password verification captive portal.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a specific product release with relevance to security professionals who use Kali Linux or Kali NetHunter, especially due to new tools, VM boot changes, and NetHunter updates. It is not urgent or high-impact for most defenders because it does not report active exploitation, a breach, or a critical patch.

04announcement
Aikido acquires Root to secure the supply chain
aikido.dev · 30 Jun 2026 14:23 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSaikidoaikido-imagesaikido-librarieslog4shellnode-jsnodesourceopenjsroot
63
In plain words

Aikido has bought Root, a company that creates fixes for security flaws in the open source software many apps rely on. This matters because attackers are using these flaws faster, while many teams are stuck choosing between risky updates, locked-down replacements, or leaving unsafe software in place. Aikido says its new Libraries and Images products will let customers keep using the software they already have with fixes added, and that important fixes for actively used flaws will also be shared back with the wider open source community.

Summary
Aikido has acquired Root and is launching Aikido Libraries and Aikido Images, which provide patched drop-in replacements for vulnerable open source libraries and container images without requiring disruptive upgrades or migrations. The company says critical fixes will also be contributed back upstream to strengthen the broader open source ecosystem.

Aikido announced that it has acquired Root, a company focused on addressing software supply chain security challenges. The article says open source powers almost every application in the world and has become a primary entry point for attackers. It also states that attackers are getting faster, with nearly a third of known vulnerabilities exploited on or before the day they are disclosed.

The article describes Root as using an agent-native, factory-like system that generates precise CVE patches for the package versions teams actually run. Aikido says this approach produces hundreds of verified CVE patches every day and does not introduce breaking changes. The article contrasts this with the common options of upgrading and risking production, migrating to a locked-down replacement, or continuing to run vulnerable software.

Aikido is bringing Root’s capability into its own products through Aikido Libraries and Aikido Images. These are described as vulnerability-free, drop-in replacement libraries and container images that patch software teams already run without migration or breaking changes. Aikido says critical fixes for actively exploited vulnerabilities will continue to go back to the open source community rather than being locked behind a paywall.

  • Aikido is positioning patching in place as an alternative to the common remediation choices of upgrading, migrating to a locked-down replacement, or continuing to run vulnerable software.
  • The article frames speed as the central supply chain security problem, citing that attackers can exploit many disclosed vulnerabilities before teams have time to patch through traditional upgrade processes.
  • Root’s approach focuses on the exact package versions customers already run, which directly targets the article’s concern that normal dependency updates can introduce unrelated changes or break production.
  • Aikido is trying to balance a commercial product model with open source ecosystem support by saying critical fixes for actively exploited vulnerabilities will still be contributed upstream.
Key Points
  • 01Aikido has acquired Root, a company focused on generating precise CVE patches for the package versions teams actually run.
  • 02The article says open source software is a primary entry point for attackers, and nearly a third of known vulnerabilities are exploited on or before the day they are disclosed.
  • 03Root uses an agent-native, factory-like system to produce hundreds of verified CVE patches every day without introducing breaking changes, according to the article.
  • 04Aikido is launching Aikido Libraries and Aikido Images as vulnerability-free, drop-in replacement libraries and container images for software teams already use.
  • 05Aikido says critical fixes for actively exploited vulnerabilities will continue to be contributed back to the open source community rather than being locked behind a paywall.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific acquisition and product launch related to open source supply chain remediation, which may be relevant to security teams evaluating patching options. It is not urgent threat intelligence, active exploitation reporting, or a technical advisory, and much of the article is vendor announcement material.

01news
CIA chief highlights major shifts in agency’s tech approach
therecord.media · 30 Jun 2026 19:05 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSawsaws-summitcenter-for-cyber-intelligencechinaciadirectorate-of-mission-systemsjohn-ratcliffeoffice-of-corporate-partnershipsunited-states
58
In plain words

CIA Director John Ratcliffe said the agency is changing how it uses and buys new technology, especially artificial intelligence, data tools, and systems that protect its information. He said the CIA has reorganized parts of the agency, sped up technology purchases from nearly three years to about six months, and created a new office to work with private companies. This matters because he described advanced artificial intelligence as powerful and risky, with decisions that can affect U.S. national security and America’s advantage over foreign competitors, especially China.

Summary
CIA Director John Ratcliffe said the agency is rapidly reshaping its technology strategy, especially around AI, data infrastructure, cybersecurity, and faster procurement. He framed frontier AI as a major national security priority and said the CIA must move faster and take more risks to maintain U.S. strategic advantage.

CIA Director John Ratcliffe publicly described recent changes at the agency as a “fundamental reshaping” of how it uses and pursues cutting-edge technology. Speaking at the AWS Summit in Washington, D.C., he said the CIA cannot wait for a risk-free approach to emerging technologies and must move quickly and aggressively. He emphasized artificial intelligence in particular, saying frontier AI models have both major potential and risk and that their capabilities could be described as akin to “digital nuclear weapons.”

Ratcliffe said AI is a domain in which the CIA must excel because algorithmic decisions affect U.S. strategic advantage and national security. He pointed to several organizational changes, including elevating the Center for Cyber Intelligence into its own mission center and transforming the Directorate of Digital Innovation into the Directorate of Mission Systems. The new directorate will focus on core functions such as cybersecurity, advanced data, and infrastructure services, rather than offensive cyber or open source duties.

The CIA is also conducting an “aggressive data sprint” to improve the discovery and use of mission data, standardize data across the agency, integrate its holdings, and train officers on new capabilities. Ratcliffe said the agency has revamped procurement and reduced the time to adopt new technologies from nearly three years to about six months, resulting in hundreds of new acquisitions. He also said the CIA created an Office of Corporate Partnerships to give private industry partners a single point of access.

  • Ratcliffe’s comparison of frontier AI to “digital nuclear weapons” shows the CIA is treating AI not just as an efficiency tool, but as a technology with strategic national security consequences.
  • The CIA’s technology changes pair internal reorganization with external outreach, combining a new mission systems directorate, faster procurement, and a single access point for private industry partners.
  • By saying the Directorate of Mission Systems will not handle offensive cyber or open source responsibilities, Ratcliffe drew a clear separation between foundational technology services and intelligence collection or operations roles.
  • The reported reduction in technology adoption time from nearly three years to roughly six months indicates that procurement speed is a central part of the CIA’s effort to become less risk-averse with emerging technologies.
Key Points
  • 01CIA Director John Ratcliffe said the agency has made recent changes that amount to a “fundamental reshaping” of how it uses and pursues cutting-edge technology.
  • 02Ratcliffe said frontier artificial intelligence models carry both major potential and risk, describing their capabilities as akin to “digital nuclear weapons.”
  • 03The CIA elevated its Center for Cyber Intelligence into its own mission center and transformed the Directorate of Digital Innovation into the Directorate of Mission Systems.
  • 04Ratcliffe said the Directorate of Mission Systems will focus on core functions such as cybersecurity, advanced data, and infrastructure services rather than offensive cyber or open source duties.
  • 05The CIA has revamped its procurement framework, shortening the time to adopt new technologies from nearly three years to roughly six months and creating an Office of Corporate Partnerships for private industry partners.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This reports a specific public update on CIA technology strategy, including AI, cybersecurity, data infrastructure, and procurement changes, but it does not describe an incident, vulnerability, active threat, breach, or technical finding. It is relevant for understanding government security priorities, but low urgency for most security professionals.

02sponsored
Lessons from the Underground: How to Combat Business Email Compromise
bleepingcomputer.com · 30 Jun 2026 14:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSaustriabigjackblackhatpakistanbusiness-email-compromisecapitadeepfakedreadeuropefinlandflaregermanyinvoice-fraudmoney-mulesneoresuoffice-365
55
In plain words

The article explains that business email scams are not just simple fake messages, but organized fraud operations where criminals break into company email or work accounts, study how payments are approved, and then send convincing fake invoices. Company leaders, finance staff, and anyone involved in buying or paying bills are especially affected, because criminals target their accounts and may even use phone calls to pressure them. The real-world risk is stolen money, made harder to spot because the fake requests can come from real company accounts and use real names, invoice details, and familiar wording.

Summary
Business Email Compromise has evolved into an organized, multi-step operation involving compromised SaaS/mail accounts, AI-generated communications, call-center pressure, and money-mule cash-out networks. Defenders should focus on protecting finance and leadership accounts, monitoring exposed credentials, and strengthening payment verification before fraudulent invoices arrive.

Business Email Compromise is described as more than a simple email scam; the article says it is part of an organized operation that can include account access, mailbox analysis, payment infrastructure, timing, and money movement. Flare researchers analyzed underground posts related to BEC from the past year and found interest in SaaS accounts such as O365, especially accounts belonging to corporate leaders and finance employees. The article says attackers use compromised mailboxes to study procurement processes, vendor communications, invoices, and financial privileges before making fraudulent requests.

The article highlights underground discussions in which threat actors focused on practical fraud questions such as when to send an invoice, how to create urgency, what mailbox information to reuse, and what mistakes can ruin an operation. It also says cash-out is a major bottleneck because attackers need reliable business bank accounts, mule networks, or cash-out partners to move stolen funds. Some posts described using call centers to pressure companies into faster payments, showing that BEC can involve phone calls as well as email.

The article also says AI is increasingly being adopted in BEC to generate realistic business correspondence, mimic writing styles, and create context-aware payment or invoice fraud messages. It states that defenders should identify likely targets such as leadership, finance staff, and procurement participants, and provide additional training for them. It also recommends paying attention to AI-generated content, call-center pressure tactics, exposed credentials, corporate domains, login portals, SaaS applications, and risky timing such as employee vacations.

  • BEC defenses should start before any suspicious invoice appears, because attackers first spend time learning the company’s payment process, approval chain, vendor relationships, and timing.
  • A second communication channel, such as a follow-up phone call, should not be treated as proof that a payment request is legitimate if the requester introduced or controls that channel.
  • Employee vacations and other timing markers can increase BEC risk because underground actors discuss waiting for moments when approvers are unavailable or processes may be disrupted.
  • Cash-out limits the success of BEC operations, so signs of mule recruitment or requests for clean business bank accounts can be early indicators of planned fraud.
Key Points
  • 01Business Email Compromise often begins with access to an organizational mailbox or business SaaS account, after which threat actors study organizational structure, financial privileges, procurement processes, internal conversations, vendor communications, and invoices.
  • 02Flare researchers found that underground actors are increasingly using AI to generate realistic business correspondence, mimic writing styles, and create context-aware payment requests or invoice fraud emails.
  • 03Threat actors highly value finance department email accounts because they can reveal accounts receivable, accounts payable, payrolls, invoices, overdue payments, and customer payment relationships.
  • 04Underground discussions described using call centers to pressure targeted businesses into finalizing fraudulent payments after fake invoices are sent.
  • 05The article states that cash-out is a major bottleneck for BEC because attackers need reliable business bank accounts, mule networks, or cash-out partners to move stolen money.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

The article provides useful observations about BEC operations, including underground discussion of AI use, call centers, and cash-out challenges, but it does not report a specific incident, active campaign, critical vulnerability, or urgent event. Because it is a broad sponsored trend and best-practice piece, it is low priority under the scoring rules.

55
In plain words

Silent Push says its analysts mapped the DriveSurge harmful software campaign, including the systems behind ClickFix, FakeUpdates, and thousands of compromised websites. The article says this matters because security teams can use the clues found in that mapping to spot connected websites and harmful systems earlier. The broader stakes are that organizations may be able to stop a break-in before it happens instead of only reacting afterward.

Summary
Silent Push says its analysts mapped the DriveSurge campaign infrastructure behind ClickFix, FakeUpdates, and compromised websites, identifying technical fingerprints defenders can use to spot related domains earlier. The piece primarily promotes Silent Push’s IOFA signals and a demo for detecting adversary infrastructure before campaigns launch.
  • The article frames pre-launch infrastructure as detectable, saying verified signals can be tied to adversary systems while they are still in staging.
  • The DriveSurge mapping connects ClickFix, FakeUpdates, and thousands of compromised websites, indicating the campaign can be analyzed through shared infrastructure rather than isolated websites.
  • The article emphasizes domain-linking fingerprints as an actionable way for defenders to find related malicious infrastructure earlier.
Key Points
  • 01Silent Push analysts mapped the DriveSurge malware campaign.
  • 02The mapping uncovered infrastructure behind ClickFix, FakeUpdates, and thousands of compromised websites.
  • 03The video explores technical fingerprints that defenders can use to identify connected domains and detect malicious infrastructure earlier.
  • 04Silent Push says IOFA gives SOC teams verified signals tied to adversary infrastructure that is still in staging before a campaign launches.
  • 05The article invites readers to book a tailored demo with Silent Push.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

The article references a specific malware campaign and related infrastructure, but the provided text is primarily a promotional blurb for a Silent Push video and demo rather than a detailed technical report. It offers little actionable detail directly in the article, so it is lower priority for security professionals to read.

04announcement
Silent Push partners with Vexelon to bring preemptive cyber defense to the Western Balkans
silentpush.com · 30 Jun 2026 09:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSalbaniacontext-graphhomeland-justiceindicators-of-future-attackirankosovomontenegronis2north-macedoniarussiasilent-pushsilent-push-preemptive-cyber-defense-platformukrainevexelonwestern-balkans
55
In plain words

Silent Push is partnering with Vexelon, a company that runs security operations for organizations in North Macedonia and Kosovo, to help spot attackers’ online setup before it is used against clients. This matters because the Western Balkans has faced repeated cyber incidents tied to wider political tensions, affecting government services, telecommunications, media, and critical infrastructure. For organizations that rely on Vexelon as their main security team, the partnership is meant to give earlier warning and reduce the chance that attacks only become visible after damage has already begun.

Summary
Silent Push has partnered with Vexelon to integrate its preemptive cyber defense platform into Vexelon’s managed SOC services across the Western Balkans. The partnership aims to help organizations detect adversary infrastructure before attacks reach their networks, especially in a region facing persistent geopolitical cyber threats.

Silent Push announced a strategic partnership with Vexelon, a cybersecurity firm providing managed security operations, incident response, and advisory services across the Western Balkans. Through the partnership, Vexelon clients gain access to the Silent Push Preemptive Cyber Defense platform within a managed SOC operation. Vexelon runs round-the-clock security operations for organizations across North Macedonia and Kosovo, covering SOC-as-a-Service, managed detection and response, attack surface management, incident response, and compliance readiness.

The article says the Western Balkans has faced targeted cyber threats tied to broader geopolitical tensions, including incidents affecting Montenegro, Albania, Kosovo, and North Macedonia. It describes attackers as setting up infrastructure quietly before operations become visible to security teams. Silent Push argues that many SOC workflows begin too late because alerts often arrive after attacker infrastructure has already been live for weeks.

Silent Push says its Context Graph maps infrastructure across DNS, WHOIS, certificates, and hosting data at internet scale. When management patterns match adversary infrastructure-building tactics, the system surfaces clusters as Indicators of Future Attack, which the article describes as verified signals that a staging ground exists before it is pointed at anyone. The article states that Vexelon’s SOC now has an average of 154 days of lead time before a campaign reaches a client’s perimeter, and that the data can support automated enrichment, triage, and response workflows.

  • The article frames the Western Balkans as a place where state-linked and geopolitically motivated cyber activity has repeatedly affected public institutions, making early visibility into attacker preparation especially relevant for regional defenders.
  • The partnership is particularly consequential because Vexelon acts as the primary security team for many clients, so any improvement in its detection pipeline directly affects organizations that may not have internal security staff to compensate.
  • Silent Push emphasizes that its signals are based on deterministic infrastructure data such as DNS, WHOIS, certificates, and hosting records, which the article says can reduce false positives compared with probabilistic scoring and noisy feeds.
  • The article connects NIS2 alignment and EU accession pressures with a practical shift in regional security buying, as organizations building security programs for the first time are turning to managed providers like Vexelon.
Key Points
  • 01Silent Push announced a strategic partnership with Vexelon to bring the Silent Push Preemptive Cyber Defense platform into Vexelon’s managed SOC operations in the Western Balkans.
  • 02Vexelon provides round-the-clock security operations for organizations across North Macedonia and Kosovo, including SOC-as-a-Service, managed detection and response, attack surface management, incident response, and compliance readiness.
  • 03The article says the Western Balkans has faced targeted cyber threats tied to geopolitical tensions, including incidents affecting Montenegro, Albania, Kosovo, and North Macedonia.
  • 04Silent Push says its Context Graph maps DNS, WHOIS, certificates, and hosting data to identify Indicators of Future Attack before adversary infrastructure is used against a client.
  • 05Silent Push states that Vexelon’s SOC now has an average of 154 days of lead time before a campaign reaches a client’s perimeter.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a company partnership announcement about integrating Silent Push’s platform into Vexelon’s managed SOC services, not a report of an active incident, vulnerability, breach, or technical finding. It may be relevant to professionals tracking Western Balkans security services or preemptive infrastructure intelligence, but it is primarily promotional and low-urgency.

05announcement
Anthropic rolls out Sonnet 5 with near-Opus 4.8 performance at a lower price
bleepingcomputer.com · 30 Jun 2026 23:13 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSanthropicbrowsecompclaude-codeclaude-fableclaude-opus-4-8claude-sonnet-5osworld-verifiedunited-statesus-government
45
In plain words

Anthropic is releasing Claude Sonnet 5, a new version of its AI assistant that can do more complex tasks like planning, coding help, research, document work, and using tools such as browsers and terminals. It is meant to be close in ability to the more expensive Opus 4.8 model while costing less, so people using Claude for work or study may get stronger help without paying as much. Free, Pro, and Max users can access it, and developers using Anthropic’s API also get lower pricing than Opus.

Summary
Anthropic is rolling out Claude Sonnet 5, a cheaper model that brings agentic planning, tool use, coding, and research capabilities much closer to its flagship Opus 4.8. It is available to Free, Pro, and Max users, with introductory API pricing below Opus.

Anthropic is rolling out Claude Sonnet 5, a lower-cost model that the company says is close to its flagship Opus 4.8 model in performance. Anthropic describes Sonnet 5 as “built to be the most agentic Sonnet model yet,” with advanced capabilities such as making plans and using tools like browsers and terminals. The article says these features were previously mostly limited to Opus 4.8, but Sonnet 5 can now do almost everything the flagship model can.

The article says Sonnet 5 is a major upgrade for users who rely on Claude for coding, research, automation, document work, and other multi-step tasks. The author reports that Sonnet 5 feels similar to Opus in planning, tool calling, verifying its own work, and handling requests to fix code. Anthropic says testers described Sonnet 5 as “much more agentic than its predecessors” and noted that it can check its own output without always being explicitly asked.

Anthropic says users can adjust effort levels between Sonnet 5 and Opus 4.8 depending on whether they want lower cost or maximum performance. Sonnet 5 launches with introductory API pricing of \(2 per million input tokens and \)10 per million output tokens through August 31, 2026, rising afterward to \(3 and \)15 respectively. Opus 4.8 is listed at \(5 per million input tokens and \)25 per million output tokens, while Sonnet 5 is available to Free, Pro, and Max subscribers.

  • Capabilities that were previously mostly limited to Opus 4.8, such as planning and tool use, are now available in the lower-cost Sonnet tier.
  • The article’s author found Sonnet 5 useful for understanding large codebases and handling “fix your code” follow-up requests, areas where they say Sonnet 4.6 was weaker.
  • Anthropic is positioning Sonnet 5 as a cost-control option for heavy Claude users who may otherwise exhaust usage faster with more expensive Opus models.
  • Sonnet 5’s introductory API pricing is only available through August 31, 2026, after which both input and output token prices increase.
Key Points
  • 01Anthropic is rolling out Claude Sonnet 5 as a cheaper model that is intended to perform close to its flagship Opus 4.8 model.
  • 02Anthropic says Sonnet 5 is built to be its most agentic Sonnet model yet, with capabilities such as planning and using tools like browsers and terminals.
  • 03The article says Sonnet 5 is a major upgrade for coding, research, automation, document work, and other multi-step tasks.
  • 04Anthropic says Sonnet 5 will launch with introductory API pricing of $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, 2026, rising afterward to $3 and $15 respectively.
  • 05Sonnet 5 is available to users on Free, Pro, and Max subscriptions.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a product release announcement for Anthropic’s Claude Sonnet 5, not a security incident, vulnerability, patch, breach, or threat actor report. It may be useful for security professionals who use Claude for coding, research, or automation, but it has low urgency from a cybersecurity risk or defense perspective.

06commercial
IOC vs IOFA: Why the Difference Matters More Than You Think
silentpush.com · 30 Jun 2026 21:00 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSfin7gartnerindicators-of-future-attacklazarus-grouppoisonseedsilent-pushsilent-push-context-graph
45
In plain words

The article explains that many security tools warn organizations after harmful online activity has already happened, while Silent Push says its system can spot signs that attackers are preparing their online setup before they use it. This matters for companies and their security teams because earlier warning could let them block attacker-controlled domains or servers before an incident begins. The broader stakes are a shift from reacting after damage starts to preventing some attacks from happening at all.

Summary
The article argues that traditional Indicators of Compromise are useful but reactive, while Silent Push’s Indicators of Future Attack aim to identify adversary infrastructure during staging so defenders can block threats before campaigns launch. Its main message is that security teams should shift from post-incident detection toward preemptive defense using earlier, higher-confidence infrastructure intelligence.

The article compares Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) with Silent Push Indicators of Future Attack (IOFA). It says IOCs are reactive data points, such as malicious domains, IP addresses, file hashes, and registry keys, that show an attack has already occurred or is underway. IOFA are described as proactive signals, including hostnames, domains, or IP addresses, that reveal adversary infrastructure during preparation before a campaign is launched.

Silent Push says IOFA are produced by its Context Graph, which continuously maps internet infrastructure and analyzes domain registrations, DNS resolutions, hosting patterns, certificate rotations, and more than 150 behavioral attributes. The article states that a Fortune 500 media and entertainment company using IOFA in SIEM workflows achieved an average early-detection lead time of 104 days, with some APT-related cases exceeding 300 days. It also says IOFA can help SOC managers, incident responders, threat intelligence analysts, and CISOs block staged infrastructure, expand investigations, and describe security value in terms of incidents prevented.

The article argues that IOCs remain useful for confirming compromise, supporting forensic investigation, and feeding detection rules, but are limited because adversaries rapidly rotate infrastructure and IOC feeds can be noisy or stale. It presents IOFA as a way to track adversary behavior patterns and connected infrastructure before indicators appear in traditional feeds. The broader significance is framed as a shift from reactive cyber defense toward Preemptive Cyber Defense, with IOFA feeding into existing SIEM, SOAR, and TIP platforms rather than replacing them.

  • The article frames the main weakness of IOC-led defense as a timing problem: defenders often begin only after a “patient zero” compromise, while adversaries may have already registered, aged, and operationalized their infrastructure.
  • Silent Push claims the most useful detection signal is not a single suspicious domain or IP address, but the repeated behavioral patterns attackers leave across domain registration, DNS, hosting, certificate rotation, and other infrastructure activity.
  • The article says a single incident indicator can be expanded through infrastructure relationships, DNS history, and WHOIS lineage to find fallback assets that attackers may use to regain access after remediation.
  • The article positions IOFA as board-relevant because its value can be described as incidents that never happened, rather than only as attacks detected after they began.
  • The article cites Gartner’s projection that adoption of Preemptive Cyber Defense solutions will grow from 5% to 35% by 2028, presenting preemptive detection as an emerging strategic shift rather than only a vendor feature.
Key Points
  • 01Indicators of Compromise are reactive data points, such as malicious domains, IP addresses, file hashes, and registry keys, that show an attack has already occurred or is actively underway.
  • 02Silent Push Indicators of Future Attack are proactive signals, including hostnames, domains, or IP addresses, that reveal where or how an attack will be launched before it happens.
  • 03Silent Push says its Context Graph analyzes domain registrations, DNS resolutions, hosting patterns, certificate rotations, and more than 150 behavioral attributes to identify adversary staging activity before campaigns are weaponized.
  • 04A Fortune 500 media and entertainment company using Silent Push IOFA in SIEM workflows achieved an average early-detection lead time of 104 days, with some APT-related lead times exceeding 300 days.
  • 05The article says IOFA can feed into existing SIEM, SOAR, and TIP platforms and are designed to work alongside existing IOC-based tools rather than replace them.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a vendor-framed discussion of broad threat intelligence concepts and preemptive defense rather than a report on a specific incident, vulnerability, campaign, or urgent finding. It may be useful for understanding Silent Push’s positioning, but it is generic and promotional, so it is low priority for security professionals.

In plain words

This Corelight article is the first in a four-part series about lessons from the Black Hat NOC, focusing on MCP and “agentic SOCs,” meaning newer ways for security teams to use automated helpers in their work. It matters because the people affected are the teams trying to watch for and respond to security problems, and the broader stakes are whether these tools can make that work more effective without creating new risks.

Summary
Corelight’s Black Hat NOC experience suggests that Model Context Protocol and agentic SOC workflows can help security teams connect tools and context more effectively, but they must be designed with strong guardrails and operational trust in mind.

The article is a Corelight piece titled “What the Black Hat NOC Taught Me About MCP & Agentic SOCs.” It is identified as Chapter 1 of a four-part series. The visible article information indicates that it discusses lessons from the Black Hat NOC in relation to MCP and agentic SOCs.

The provided article text does not include readable details about a specific incident, affected organizations, or concrete outcomes. It also does not provide readable technical details beyond the named focus areas of MCP and agentic SOCs. Based on the visible information alone, the significance is that the article frames Black Hat NOC experience as a source of lessons for these security operations topics.

08announcement
Distology becomes Value-Added Distributor of Radiflow in the Benelux
mspbusiness.com · 30 Jun 2026 16:10 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSbeneluxdistologyradiflowunited-kingdom
35
In plain words

Distology will now distribute Radiflow’s security products in the Benelux region, after the companies had already worked together in the United Kingdom. Radiflow’s tools are meant for industrial organizations such as energy, utilities, manufacturing, transport and chemical companies, especially medium-sized ones that need help protecting their networks. This matters because these organizations run services and operations people rely on, and the goal is to spot problems earlier, reduce downtime and keep operations running.

Summary
Distology is adding Radiflow’s OT cybersecurity platform to its Benelux portfolio as a value-added distributor. The partnership will give resellers and system integrators local access to Radiflow’s threat detection, risk management and compliance tools for industrial and critical infrastructure environments.

Distology has added Radiflow to its Benelux portfolio as a Value-Added Distributor. Radiflow provides OT cybersecurity solutions for industrial sectors including energy, utilities, manufacturing, transport and chemicals. The partnership follows an earlier joint trajectory between the companies in the United Kingdom.

Radiflow says its platform provides real-time threat detection based on risk analysis, automated risk management and support for compliance requirements. The company states that its approach combines multiple tools and manual processes into one platform, giving OT security teams a full view of industrial networks. Radiflow expects this to help organizations detect threats earlier, limit downtime and maintain operational continuity.

Through Distology, Radiflow’s solutions will be available to resellers and system integrators in the Benelux. Distology will support partners with knowledge, training and tools for implementing Radiflow technology at end customers. Radiflow is specifically targeting medium-sized organizations in critical infrastructure and industry that need OT cybersecurity but do not have the resources of large enterprises.

  • The Benelux partnership is not a first-time collaboration, because the article says it follows an earlier joint trajectory between Distology and Radiflow in the United Kingdom.
  • Radiflow’s stated target is not large enterprises but medium-sized critical infrastructure and industrial organizations that need OT security while lacking the resources of bigger companies.
  • The distribution model is central to the announcement because Radiflow expects Distology’s reseller and system integrator network to make its tools more accessible through local expertise and implementation partners.
Key Points
  • 01Distology has added Radiflow to its Benelux portfolio as a Value-Added Distributor.
  • 02Radiflow provides OT cybersecurity solutions for industrial sectors including energy, utilities, manufacturing, transport and chemicals.
  • 03Radiflow says its platform offers real-time threat detection based on risk analysis, automated risk management and support for compliance requirements.
  • 04Through Distology, Radiflow’s solutions will be available to resellers and system integrators in the Benelux.
  • 05Distology will support partners with knowledge, training and tools for implementing Radiflow technology at end customers.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is a business partnership announcement about Distology distributing Radiflow’s OT cybersecurity products in the Benelux, not a threat report, breach, vulnerability, or technical analysis. It may be relevant to channel partners or buyers in that region, but it has low urgency for most security professionals.

09announcement
AppOmni at BlackHat 2026
appomni.com · 30 Jun 2026 20:38 UTC openai-codex / gpt-5.5
TAGSappomnibella-mayerblack-hat-2026ciso-summitcory-michallas-vegasmelissa-ruzzited-wilsonunited-statesw-hotel
25
In plain words

AppOmni is inviting people attending Black Hat 2026 in Las Vegas to private meetings with its experts about protecting business software, managing the use of artificial intelligence, and reducing risk. The article is aimed at business and security leaders, current AppOmni customers, and people attending the CISO Summit. It matters because the company says organizations in fields like healthcare, financial services, and legal work face serious challenges in keeping their software and AI tools safe without adding too much extra work.

Summary
AppOmni will host private executive briefings at Black Hat 2026 in Las Vegas to discuss SaaS security, AI governance, and risk reduction with its AI and leadership experts. Attendees and customers can schedule meetings or connect at the CISO Summit for insights and exclusive swag.

AppOmni will host private executive briefings at Black Hat 2026 on August 5–6, 2026, from 8:00 a.m. to 7:00 p.m. PT. The meetings will take place at the W Hotel Penthouse in the Penthouse II City View Suite. The article says 45-minute meeting slots are available for focused conversations away from the show floor.

The briefings are focused on securing SaaS environments, governing AI usage, and reducing organizational risk. AppOmni says attendees can meet one-on-one with Melissa Ruzzi, Senior Director of AI, Ted Wilson, Principal AI Security Product Manager, and members of the AppOmni leadership team. The article says discussions may cover priorities and operational challenges, including identifying high-risk AI agents and apps and reducing manual work with autonomous AI.

AppOmni also says it will attend the CISO Summit on Tuesday, August 4, at the Four Seasons Ballroom, Level 2, with AppOmni CISO Cory Michal in attendance. The company says it will share insights from work securing enterprises in healthcare, financial services, legal, and other highly regulated industries. Current AppOmni customers are invited to connect at the show and claim exclusive swag in exchange for sharing their story.

  • The executive briefings are positioned away from the show floor in a private hotel suite, which suggests AppOmni is targeting focused conversations with senior decision-makers rather than general booth traffic.
  • AppOmni is emphasizing AI governance alongside SaaS security, showing that its Black Hat 2026 messaging ties traditional business application protection to emerging concerns around AI agents and AI usage.
  • The article gives a direct scheduling path through Bella Mayer’s email, making the primary action for interested attendees to reserve a 45-minute meeting rather than visit a public booth.
  • AppOmni is using the CISO Summit and customer-only swag as separate engagement tracks, indicating different outreach approaches for security leaders and existing customers.
Key Points
  • 01AppOmni will host executive briefings during Black Hat 2026 on August 5–6, 2026, from 8:00 a.m. to 7:00 p.m. PT at the W Hotel Penthouse in the Penthouse II City View Suite.
  • 02The briefings offer 45-minute private meeting slots focused on SaaS security, AI governance, and risk reduction.
  • 03Attendees can meet one-on-one with Melissa Ruzzi, Ted Wilson, and AppOmni leadership to discuss priorities and operational challenges.
  • 04AppOmni says its enterprise customers are securing SaaS environments, governing AI usage, identifying high-risk AI agents and apps, and reducing manual work with autonomous AI.
  • 05AppOmni will also attend the CISO Summit on Tuesday, August 4, where AppOmni CISO Cory Michal will be present.
OPEN SOURCE ↗
JUSTIFICATION

This is primarily an event promotion for AppOmni meetings and CISO Summit attendance at Black Hat 2026. It does not report a security incident, technical finding, advisory, vulnerability, or active threat, so it is low priority for security professionals unless they plan to attend the event.

↑ TOP

In this article

Glossary
.NET product
.NET is a Microsoft software framework used to build and run applications, especially on Windows.
.top TLD concept
.top TLD is an internet top-level domain ending that can be used for websites and is sometimes monitored for abuse.
/asi technique
/asi is a URL path or command-like string that would need context to know what system or action it refers to.
/asu technique
/asu is a URL path or command-like string that would need context to know what system or action it refers to.
/bin/bash tool
/bin/bash is the common file path for the Bash command-line shell on Unix and Linux systems.
/etc/passwd concept
/etc/passwd is a Linux file that lists local user accounts and basic account information.
8Base threat-actor
8Base is a ransomware and data-extortion group known for targeting organizations and leaking stolen data.
Access Token Manipulation: Make and Impersonate Token technique
Access Token Manipulation: Make and Impersonate Token is a MITRE ATT&CK technique where attackers create or use security tokens to act as another user.
also: T1134.003
accounts payable concept
Accounts payable is the business function that handles money an organization owes to suppliers or vendors.
accounts receivable concept
Accounts receivable is the business function that tracks money owed to an organization by customers.
ACH fraud technique
ACH fraud is the abuse of the Automated Clearing House bank transfer system to steal or redirect money.
actively exploited vulnerabilities vulnerability
Actively exploited vulnerabilities are security flaws that attackers are currently using in real-world attacks.
advanced data concept
Advanced data is a broad phrase for complex or high-value data used for deeper analysis or specialized missions.
Adversa AI organization
Adversa AI is a company focused on AI security, adversarial machine learning, and testing AI systems.
Aflac organization
Aflac is an insurance company that provides supplemental health and life insurance products.
Aflac Japan organization
Aflac Japan is the Japanese business of Aflac, an insurance company known for supplemental insurance products.
agent-native approach concept
An agent-native approach designs software around autonomous AI agents rather than only traditional user commands.
also: agent-native
agentic concept
Agentic describes an AI system that can take initiative, plan steps, use tools, and work toward a goal with less constant human direction.
Agentic AI concept
Agentic AI is artificial intelligence designed to take actions and pursue goals with limited human step-by-step control.
agentic search technique
Agentic search is when an AI system actively plans and carries out multiple searches or steps to find an answer instead of doing a single simple lookup.
agentic sessions concept
Agentic sessions are periods where an AI agent works through tasks by making decisions and taking actions on a user's behalf.
Agents concept
Agents are software components or AI systems that can perform tasks or make decisions on behalf of a user or service.
AI concept
AI, or artificial intelligence, is technology that can perform tasks that normally require human reasoning or decision-making.
also: AI-powered BEC, AI-powered BEC attacks
AI assistant product
An AI assistant is software that uses artificial intelligence to answer questions or help with tasks.
AI-generated content concept
AI-generated content is text, images, audio, or video created by artificial intelligence systems.
also: AI-powered artifacts
AI-powered browsers product
AI-powered browsers are web browsers that include artificial intelligence features to summarize, search, automate, or assist with web tasks.
also: AI browser, AI browsers, agentic browser products
Aider tool
Aider is an AI coding assistant that helps developers edit code from the command line.
Aikido product
A security platform that scans applications, cloud infrastructure, and code repositories for vulnerabilities and misconfigurations.
Aikido Images product
Aikido Images is a product or service related to container or software images from Aikido Security.
Aikido Libraries product
Aikido Libraries is a product or service related to software libraries from Aikido Security.
AirDrop protocol
AirDrop is Apple’s nearby file-sharing feature for sending photos, documents, and other files between Apple devices.
Aisuru malware
Aisuru is a malware or botnet name referenced in security reporting.
algorithmic decision concept
An algorithmic decision is a choice or recommendation made by software using rules, data, or machine-learning models.
Aliyun Agent tool
Aliyun Agent is a cloud management agent associated with Alibaba Cloud systems.
Amaryllis Fox Kennedy other
Amaryllis Fox Kennedy is a public figure and former CIA officer.
Android product
Android is Google's mobile operating system used on many phones, tablets, and other devices.
Android Accessibility Service technique
Android Accessibility Service is an Android feature meant to help users with disabilities, but malware can abuse it to read screens, click buttons, and control apps.
Android Debug Bridge tool
Android Debug Bridge is a command-line tool developers use to communicate with and control Android devices from a computer.
Ansible tool
Ansible is an automation tool used to configure systems, deploy software, and manage IT infrastructure.
anthropic
Anthropic is an AI company known for the Claude family of AI assistants.
Anthropic Claude product
Anthropic Claude is an AI chatbot and assistant made by the company Anthropic.
Apache Airflow product
Apache Airflow is an open-source platform for scheduling and managing data workflows.
Apache CouchDB product
Apache CouchDB is an open-source database product that stores data in documents and can be accessed over the web.
API protocol
An API is a way for one program to ask another program or service for data or actions.
App Store product
Apple's digital marketplace for downloading and installing applications on iOS and macOS devices.
apple
Apple is a technology company that makes iPhones, Macs, iPads, and related software and services.
AppOmni organization
AppOmni is a cybersecurity company focused on securing software-as-a-service applications.
arsenal-ng tool
arsenal-ng is a command-line security tool that helps penetration testers search and reuse commands.
artificial intelligence concept
Artificial intelligence is technology that lets computers perform tasks that usually need human-like thinking or pattern recognition.
AssertionConsumerServiceURL protocol
AssertionConsumerServiceURL is a SAML setting that tells an identity provider where to send a user's login response after authentication.
also: ACSURL, acs
Attack Chain concept
An attack chain is the sequence of steps an attacker follows to break into a system and reach their goal.
attack surface management concept
Attack surface management is the process of finding, tracking, and reducing the systems and entry points attackers could target.
attacker-supplied Python code technique
Attacker-supplied Python code is Python code provided by an attacker and run by a system, often leading to compromise if not safely controlled.
also: Python code
attackers concept
Attackers are people or groups who try to break into systems, steal data, or cause harm online.
Authentication Flaw vulnerability
An authentication flaw is a weakness in how a system checks a user's identity, which may let the wrong person get access.
AuthnRequest protocol
AuthnRequest is a SAML message that asks an identity provider to authenticate a user.
also: samlp:AuthnRequest
Automation concept
Automation uses software to perform tasks with little or no human intervention.
AWS product
AWS, or Amazon Web Services, is Amazon's cloud computing platform for hosting applications, storing data, and running online services.
AWS Summit organization
AWS Summit is an Amazon Web Services event where people learn about cloud technology, products, and practices.
AWS-LC tool
AWS-LC is Amazon Web Services’ cryptographic library used to provide encryption and security functions in software.
Azure AD product
Azure AD, now called Microsoft Entra ID, is Microsoft’s cloud identity service for managing users, logins, and access.
Azure AI Foundry product
Azure AI Foundry is a Microsoft Azure platform for building, deploying, and managing AI applications and models.
Base64 concept
A method to convert binary data into text using 64 characters, often used to obfuscate malware scripts or passwords.
also: base64-encoded
BaseZipInstaller malware
BaseZipInstaller is a named malware or installer component referenced in security reporting.
Bash tool
A widely-used command-line shell and scripting language on Linux, macOS, and Windows Subsystem for Linux.
BDSubWiz.exe tool
BDSubWiz.exe is a Windows executable associated with Bitdefender software and may appear in endpoint investigations.
Bella Mayer other
Bella Mayer is a named person referenced in the source material.
Benelux other
Benelux is a regional grouping of Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg.
Bigjack technique
Bigjack is a named attack technique, malware, or tool referenced in security reporting.
Bill Pulte other
Bill Pulte is a U.S. business and public figure.
BioShock-themed puzzle game product
A BioShock-themed puzzle game is a puzzle activity styled around the BioShock video game series.
BioShocking technique
BioShocking is a named attack technique or campaign referenced in security reporting.
also: BioShocking attack
Bitdefender ConnectAgent tool
Bitdefender ConnectAgent is a Bitdefender software component used to connect endpoint security products to management services.
Black Basta threat-actor
Black Basta is a ransomware and extortion group known for attacking organizations and stealing data.
Black Hat organization
Black Hat is a major cybersecurity conference series where researchers, companies, and practitioners discuss security research and tools.
Black Hat 2026 organization
Black Hat 2026 is a cybersecurity conference event in the Black Hat conference series.
Black Hat NOC organization
Black Hat NOC is the network operations center that monitors and secures the network at Black Hat security conferences.
Blackfield threat-actor
Blackfield is a ransomware or cybercriminal group name referenced in security reporting.
blackhatpakistan threat-actor
blackhatpakistan is a named threat actor, handle, or underground identity referenced in security reporting.
Blackpoint Cyber organization
Blackpoint Cyber is a cybersecurity company that provides managed detection and response and related security services.
BlueHammer threat-actor
BlueHammer is a named threat actor, malware, or tool referenced in security reporting.
Brand Impersonation technique
Brand impersonation is when attackers pretend to be a trusted company or service to trick people.
brazil other
Brazil is a country in South America.
breaking changes concept
Breaking changes are software updates that can stop existing code, integrations, or workflows from working correctly.
also: unrelated changes
Brokewell Android Loader malware
Brokewell Android Loader is Android malware used to install or deliver other malicious components.
Brokewell Banking Trojan malware
Brokewell Banking Trojan is Android malware designed to steal banking credentials and control infected phones.
BrowseComp organization
BrowseComp is a benchmark used to evaluate how well AI systems can browse the web and find information.
browser search settings concept
Browser search settings control which search engine a browser uses and how searches are handled.
also: search settings
browser's control agent tool
A browser's control agent is a component or AI agent that can operate a web browser on a user's behalf.
also: AI agents, agent
browsers tool
Browsers are apps like Chrome, Safari, or Firefox that let people visit and interact with websites.
browsing information concept
Browsing information is data about websites a person visits and what they do in a web browser.
business bank accounts concept
Business bank accounts are financial accounts used by organizations to receive, store, and send money.
also: business bank account
business-email-compromise technique
Business email compromise is a scam where attackers use or impersonate business email accounts to trick people into sending money or data.
also: BEC, email fraud
California other
California is a U.S. state often referenced in technology, privacy, and cybersecurity policy.
call center organization
A call center is a team or service that handles large volumes of customer phone calls or support requests.
also: call centers, support call centers
canada
Canada is a North American country often referenced in cybersecurity reporting as a target, regulator, or law-enforcement partner.
Canadian Centre for Cyber Security organization
The Canadian Centre for Cyber Security is Canada's national authority for cyber security guidance and incident response.
Capita organization
Capita is a business services and outsourcing company that has appeared in cybersecurity reporting.
cash-out services concept
Cash-out services help criminals turn stolen funds, accounts, or digital assets into spendable money.
also: cash-out
CAT:HPHISH concept
CAT:HPHISH is a security detection or classification label commonly used to mark suspected phishing activity.
CEH concept
CEH, or Certified Ethical Hacker, is a cybersecurity certification focused on ethical hacking concepts and tools.
Center for Cyber Intelligence organization
The Center for Cyber Intelligence is a CIA organization focused on cyber intelligence work.
Certbot tool
Certbot is a tool that helps websites automatically get and renew free TLS certificates from Let’s Encrypt.
certificates concept
Certificates are digital documents used to prove the identity of websites, apps, devices, or people and support encrypted connections.
ChatGPT Atlas product
ChatGPT Atlas is an AI-powered browser product associated with ChatGPT.
also: Atlas
chattr tool
chattr is a Linux command that changes special file attributes, such as making a file harder to modify or delete.
Check Point Research organization
Check Point Research is the threat intelligence and research team of cybersecurity company Check Point.
Checkmarx organization
Checkmarx is a cybersecurity company known for tools that scan software code for security vulnerabilities.
china
China is a country often referenced in cybersecurity reporting involving technology, espionage, regulation, and threat activity.
Chrome product
A popular web browser developed by Google, frequently targeted by exploit kits and phishing campaigns.
Chrome Web Store product
The Chrome Web Store is Google’s marketplace for Chrome browser extensions and themes.
chrome_settings_overrides technique
chrome_settings_overrides is a browser-extension setting that can change Chrome defaults such as the homepage or search provider.
Chromium product
Chromium is the open-source browser project that underlies Google Chrome and several other browsers.
also: Chromium browser
Chromium-based browsers product
Chromium-based browsers are web browsers built on the same open-source foundation as Google Chrome.
CIA organization
The CIA, or Central Intelligence Agency, is a U.S. intelligence agency focused on foreign intelligence and covert operations.
also: spy agency, agency
cisa organization
CISA is the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, which helps protect critical infrastructure and shares security guidance.
CISA BOD 26-04 other
CISA BOD 26-04 is a U.S. government binding operational directive referenced in cybersecurity policy or compliance reporting.
CISO Summit organization
CISO Summit is an event or meeting for chief information security officers and other security leaders.
CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security organization
CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security is a German research organization focused on cybersecurity and privacy.
Citrix organization
Citrix is a company known for remote access, virtualization, and application delivery products.
Citrix NetScaler product
Citrix NetScaler is a product line for application delivery, load balancing, and secure remote access, now commonly branded as NetScaler.
also: NetScaler, Citrix NetScalers, Citrix NetScaler devices
CitrixBleed vulnerability
CitrixBleed is the nickname for a serious Citrix NetScaler vulnerability that could leak session tokens and help attackers hijack access.
also: CitrixBleed To Infinity And Beyond
Cl0p malware
Cl0p is a ransomware family and criminal brand associated with attacks that encrypt or steal data to extort victims.
Cl0p Ransomware Operation threat-actor
The Cl0p ransomware operation is a cybercriminal group known for stealing data and using ransomware or extortion to demand payment from victims.
claude
Claude is Anthropic’s family of AI assistants and language models.
Claude browser extension product
Claude browser extension is a browser add-on that connects Anthropic's Claude AI assistant to web browsing features.
Claude Chrome plugin tool
A Claude Chrome plugin would be a browser extension that connects the Claude AI assistant to Google Chrome.
also: Chrome plugin
Claude Sonnet 3.5 product
Claude Sonnet 3.5 is an Anthropic AI language model used for tasks like writing, coding, analysis, and answering questions.
Claude Sonnet 3.6 product
Claude Sonnet 3.6 is a named Anthropic Claude AI model version used for language, coding, and reasoning tasks.
Claude Sonnet 3.7 product
Claude Sonnet 3.7 is an Anthropic Claude AI model used for tasks such as writing, coding, reasoning, and analysis.
Claude Sonnet 5 product
Claude Sonnet 5 is a named Anthropic Claude AI model or model version used for language, coding, and reasoning tasks.
also: Sonnet 5
claude-code
Claude Code is Anthropic’s AI coding assistant for helping developers write and manage software projects.
claude-fable product
claude-fable is a model or system identifier that appears to refer to a Claude-related AI model variant.
claude-opus-4-8 product
claude-opus-4-8 is a model identifier that appears to refer to an Opus 4.8 Claude-style AI model.
clickfix
ClickFix is a social-engineering tactic that tricks users into copying and running malicious commands to “fix” a fake problem.
Cline tool
Cline is an AI coding assistant tool that can help developers write and modify code from an editor.
cloud APIs protocol
Cloud APIs are interfaces that let software manage or use cloud services such as storage, servers, identity, or databases.
coding concept
Coding is writing instructions in a programming language so a computer can perform tasks.
Comet product
Comet is a named product, tool, or threat component referenced in security reporting.
Cometjacking technique
Cometjacking is a named attack technique referenced in security reporting.
command injection technique
Command injection is an attack where someone tricks an application into running system commands chosen by the attacker.
ConfuserEx tool
ConfuserEx is a .NET code obfuscation tool that can make programs harder to analyze and is sometimes abused by malware authors.
content-based detection systems tool
Content-based detection systems look at the contents of files, messages, or traffic to find suspicious or prohibited material.
context checks technique
Context checks are tests a system performs to make sure a request or action matches expected conditions before allowing it.
Context Graph concept
Context Graph is a security analysis approach or product feature that maps relationships between users, devices, activity, and risk to provide investigation context.
Continue tool
Continue is an open-source AI coding assistant that integrates with developer tools and editors.
Control Flow Guard technique
Control Flow Guard is a Windows security feature that helps stop some attacks by blocking unexpected jumps in a program’s execution.
Corelight organization
Corelight is a cybersecurity company that provides network detection and response products.
corporate email accounts concept
Corporate email accounts are work email accounts provided by an organization to its employees or teams.
also: organizational mailbox, company mailboxes, compromised mailbox
Cory Michal other
Cory Michal is a named person referenced in the source material.
CountLoader malware
CountLoader is a named malware loader referenced in security reporting.
credentials concept
Credentials are login details or access tokens that prove a user or system is allowed to access something.
critical account passwords concept
Critical account passwords are passwords for high-value accounts whose compromise could seriously harm an organization.
Critical Patch Update concept
Oracle’s Critical Patch Update is a regular bundle of security fixes for Oracle products.
cron persistence technique
Cron persistence is a technique where attackers use scheduled Linux tasks to restart malware or commands automatically.
cross-tenant chats concept
Cross-tenant chats are conversations or data exchanges between users or systems that belong to different organizations’ cloud environments.
crypto-clipper malware
A crypto-clipper is malware that changes copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses so payments go to an attacker.
cursor
Cursor is an AI-assisted code editor built for software developers.
CVE vulnerability
A CVE is a public identifier used to track a specific known software vulnerability.
also: CVEs
CVE patches vulnerability
CVE patches are software fixes for vulnerabilities that have been assigned CVE tracking identifiers.
CVSS concept
CVSS is a standard scoring system used to rate how severe a software vulnerability is.
Cybersecurity concept
Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting computers, networks, software, and data from digital attacks or misuse.
Cyble organization
Cyble is a cybersecurity company that provides threat intelligence and digital risk monitoring.
Cyentia Institute organization
Cyentia Institute is a research organization that studies cybersecurity data, risk, and measurement.
data sprint concept
A data sprint is a short, focused effort where people rapidly collect, clean, analyze, or organize data for a specific goal.
also: aggressive data sprint
data standardization concept
Data standardization means putting data into consistent formats so it can be compared, shared, and analyzed more easily.
data theft concept
Data theft is the stealing of information from a system or service.
dead drop resolver technique
A dead drop resolver is a technique malware uses to find its command server by retrieving hidden instructions from public online locations.
Declarative Net Request protocol
Declarative Net Request is a browser-extension system that lets extensions block or modify web requests using preset rules.
deepfake concept
A deepfake is fake audio, video, or imagery made with AI to make someone appear to say or do something they did not.
deepfake items concept
Deepfake items are AI-made or AI-altered media that can make people appear to say or do things they did not.
default search provider concept
A default search provider is the search engine a browser uses automatically when someone searches from the address bar.
Defused Cyber organization
Defused Cyber is a cybersecurity organization or service name associated with cyber threat intelligence and security research.
dependency update technique
A dependency update replaces a software component an application relies on with a newer version.
DevTools protocol protocol
The DevTools protocol is an interface that lets tools inspect and control Chromium-based browsers for debugging or automation.
digital nuclear weapons concept
Digital nuclear weapons is a metaphor for extremely powerful cyber capabilities that could cause major strategic damage.
Directorate of Digital Innovation organization
The Directorate of Digital Innovation is a CIA directorate focused on digital technology, cyber, data, and innovation capabilities.
Directorate of Mission Systems organization
The Directorate of Mission Systems is an organizational unit focused on technology systems that support mission operations.
Distology organization
Distology is a technology distribution company referenced in security and IT channel contexts.
Djinn Stealer malware
Djinn Stealer is a named information-stealing malware family referenced in security reporting.
DKIM protocol
DKIM is an email signing method that helps verify that a message was authorized by a domain and was not changed after signing.
DLL sideloading technique
DLL sideloading is an attack where malware places a fake DLL where a trusted Windows program will load it.
also: DLL sideloading technique
DMARC protocol
DMARC is an email policy check that uses SPF or DKIM alignment to help determine whether a message claiming to come from a domain should be trusted.
DNR permissions concept
DNR permissions let a browser extension use Declarative Net Request rules to block, redirect, or modify web traffic.
DNS protocol
DNS is the internet system that turns human-readable names like example.com into the numeric addresses computers use to find websites and services.
document work concept
Document work means creating, editing, reviewing, summarizing, or organizing written files.
domain spoofing technique
Domain spoofing is making a website or email address look like it belongs to a trusted domain to deceive people.
Donald Trump other
Donald Trump is a U.S. political figure and former president.
Dread product
Dread is a dark-web forum often discussed in cybercrime and underground-market reporting.
DriveSurge tool
DriveSurge is a named tool, campaign, or malware-like entity referenced in security reporting.
drop-in replacement libraries concept
Drop-in replacement libraries are software components designed to replace another library without requiring major code changes.
also: drop-in replacement libraries and container images
DuckDNS.org product
DuckDNS.org is a dynamic DNS service that can map changing internet addresses to easier-to-use domain names.
dynamic wallet substitution technique
Dynamic wallet substitution is a theft technique that swaps a cryptocurrency address at the moment a victim is about to send funds.
email conversation chains concept
Email conversation chains are back-and-forth email threads that attackers may hijack to make scams look trustworthy.
email impersonation technique
Email impersonation is when an attacker sends messages that appear to come from a trusted person or organization.
emerging technologies concept
Emerging technologies are new or rapidly developing technologies that may create new opportunities or risks.
environment variables concept
Environment variables are named settings stored by an operating system or runtime that programs can read while they run.
EtherHiding technique
EtherHiding is a technique where attackers hide malicious code or instructions in blockchain smart contracts.
europe
Europe is a continent often referenced in cybersecurity reporting involving regulations, victims, threat activity, or law enforcement.
Everest threat-actor
Everest is a ransomware or extortion group name referenced in security reporting.
explicit user confirmation technique
Explicit user confirmation means requiring the user to clearly approve an action before it happens.
explorer.exe product
The Windows process that provides the desktop, taskbar, and file browser windows.
extension ID concept
An extension ID is a unique identifier assigned to a browser extension.
Extortion Attacks technique
Extortion attacks are cyberattacks where criminals pressure victims to pay money, often by threatening to leak stolen data or keep systems disrupted.
Fable product
Fable is a named product, model, or project referenced in AI or technology reporting.
fake mobile apps technique
Fake mobile apps are malicious or deceptive phone apps that pretend to be legitimate in order to steal data, money, or access.
fakeupdates malware
FakeUpdates is another name for SocGholish, malware delivered through fake browser update prompts.
Federal Civilian Executive Branch organization
Federal Civilian Executive Branch refers to U.S. federal civilian agencies that are part of the executive branch.
Fellou product
Fellou appears to be a software product or company, but I cannot define it confidently.
fifa
FIFA is the international governing body for soccer and organizer of the FIFA World Cup.
FIFA World Cup 2026 other
The FIFA World Cup 2026 is a major international soccer tournament planned for 2026.
FIN7 threat-actor
FIN7 is a financially motivated cybercriminal threat group known for targeting businesses, especially in retail, hospitality, and payment environments.
finance department organization
A finance department is the part of an organization that manages money, payments, budgets, and accounting.
also: financial department
Flare organization
Flare is a cybersecurity company that monitors dark web, cybercrime, and exposed-data sources for threats.
also: Flare researchers, Flare's platform
flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd product
flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd appears to be a Chrome extension identifier referenced in security reporting.
forged token technique
A forged token is a fake or manipulated digital credential used to trick a system into granting access.
foundation concept
In technology, a foundation is a base layer, framework, or model that other systems are built on top of.
frontier artificial intelligence models concept
Frontier artificial intelligence models are cutting-edge AI systems with especially advanced capabilities compared with current mainstream models.
also: frontier AI models
gartner organization
Gartner is a research and advisory company that publishes reports about technology and cybersecurity markets.
Genspark product
Genspark appears to be an AI-related product or company, but I cannot define it confidently.
Genspark Browser product
Genspark Browser appears to be an AI-focused web browser product.
germany
Germany is a European country often referenced in cybersecurity reporting as a target, regulator, or law-enforcement partner.
GitHub product
GitHub is a platform for storing, managing, and collaborating on software code.
GitHub MCP Server tool
GitHub MCP Server is a tool that lets AI systems use the Model Context Protocol to interact with GitHub services.
GitHub repository tool
A GitHub repository is an online project folder on GitHub that stores code, files, history, and collaboration settings.
Glitch SPY malware
Glitch SPY is a malware family or spying tool referenced in security reporting.
Gmail product
Gmail is Google’s email service.
also: Gmail account
GNOME product
GNOME is a popular graphical desktop environment used on Linux and other Unix-like systems.
GoDaddy organization
GoDaddy is a company that sells domain names, web hosting, and related internet services.
Google organization
Google is a technology company that provides online services, including email-related services.
Google API protocol
A Google API is an interface that lets software connect to and use Google services programmatically.
Google Chrome product
Google Chrome is a widely used web browser made by Google.
Google Desktop Search product
Google Desktop Search was a Google product that indexed files and content on a computer so users could search them locally.
also: Google Desktop
Google Gemini product
Google Gemini is Google’s family of AI models and assistant products.
Google Play product
Google Play is Google's official app store and digital content store for Android devices.
Google Workspace Migration for Microsoft Outlook tool
Google Workspace Migration for Microsoft Outlook is a Google tool for moving email, calendar, and contact data from Outlook to Google Workspace.
also: GWMMO
Google Workspace Sync for Microsoft Outlook tool
Google Workspace Sync for Microsoft Outlook is a Google tool that lets Outlook work with Google Workspace email, calendar, and contacts.
GoogleDesktop.exe tool
GoogleDesktop.exe is a Windows executable associated with the old Google Desktop Search product and may appear in process or file investigations.
Goose tool
Goose is a named software or AI tool referenced in technical reporting.
GuardFall technique
GuardFall is a named attack technique or tool referenced in AI-agent security reporting.
harvest-now-decrypt-later concept
harvest-now-decrypt-later is the strategy of stealing encrypted data now so it can be decrypted later when stronger attack methods become available.
headless mode technique
Headless mode runs a browser or program without showing a visible window, often for automation.
helpdesk impersonation technique
Helpdesk impersonation is when an attacker pretends to be IT support to trick someone into giving access or resetting credentials.
helpers module tool
A helpers module is a piece of software code that contains reusable support functions used by other parts of a program.
also: helper modules
Hermes malware
Hermes is a malware family name used in security reporting, including for ransomware seen in past attacks.
hidden Telegram command handlers technique
Hidden Telegram command handlers are concealed pieces of bot code that listen for Telegram commands and trigger actions, possibly for malicious control.
Homeland Justice threat-actor
Homeland Justice is a threat-actor group referenced in security reporting.
honeypots tool
Honeypots are intentionally exposed or monitored systems used to observe attacker behavior.
Horizon3.ai organization
Horizon3.ai is a cybersecurity company known for automated penetration testing and attack simulation tools.
hosting data concept
